title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper investigates whether CLIP’s strong zero-shot and OOD performance on ImageNet variants is primarily driven by high train–test image similarity in its massive LAION pre-training data, or whether sheer scale and diversity play the dominant role. To this end, the authors first define a perceptual similarity metric using pre-trained CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings and show that ImageNet-based OOD benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch) have a higher density of near-duplicates in LAION than the ImageNet-1k training set does.  

They then conduct two complementary large-scale interventions:  
1. Iterative pruning of the LAION dataset by removing images most similar to each OOD test set, observing a steady drop in zero-shot accuracy as overlap is reduced.  
2. A “similarity-gap” pruning procedure that removes just enough LAION images so that the distribution of minimum distances to the OOD set matches that of ImageNet-1k. Retraining CLIP on these pruned subsets yields only marginal performance drops across six standard ImageNet OOD benchmarks.  

An additional outcome is the identification of a 100 M-image “core” subset of LAION that preserves nearly full OOD performance, suggesting practical data-efficiency gains for future work.  

Strengths  
• Clear motivation and writing. The paper is well structured, with each experiment’s rationale and methodology precisely described, and supported by helpful figures and pseudocode.  
• Novel similarity-gap framework. Introducing a formal metric for train-test overlap and a surgical pruning intervention represents a creative and principled approach distinct from prior robustness analyses.  
• Rigorous large-scale retraining. The authors retrain CLIP from scratch on multiple pruned splits and six OOD benchmarks, lending credibility to the central claim.  
• Insightful findings. Results convincingly show that removing all highly similar samples causes only minor accuracy degradation, underscoring that CLIP’s generalization owes more to scale and diversity than to accidental near-duplicates. The discovery of a 100 M-image core subset is a valuable resource for the community.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Limited to ImageNet variants. All experiments focus on ImageNet-based benchmarks. Including other OOD datasets such as iWildCam, FMoW, or domain-specific tasks (e.g., retrieval or segmentation) would strengthen the generality of the conclusions.  
2. Single embedding metric. The reliance on CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to quantify similarity may bias results toward this model’s notion of perceptual overlap. Evaluating alternative embeddings (e.g., CLIP ViT-L/14, self-supervised ResNet features) or even text-based caption similarity could assess sensitivity to the choice of metric.  
3. Sensitivity to thresholds and pruning variants. The exact similarity thresholds and pruning order can influence which images are removed. A systematic ablation of these hyperparameters, along with an analysis of how smaller threshold changes affect OOD performance, would clarify robustness.  
4. Lack of statistical variance estimates. The paper reports point estimates of accuracy drops but does not include confidence intervals or multiple retraining runs. Providing error bars or statistical significance tests would help assess the reliability of the observed marginal performance changes.  
5. Computational and reproducibility details. The scale of pruning billions of image pairs is nontrivial. More explicit information on computational cost, exact pruning thresholds, and implementation pipelines (e.g., hardware used, runtime) would benefit practitioners aiming to replicate or extend this work.  

Questions for the Authors  
1. Have you evaluated how the 100 M-image core subset performs on non-ImageNet tasks or with other foundation models (e.g., ALIGN, BASIC)?  
2. What is the impact of including or excluding ImageNet-1k captions when constructing pruned LAION splits?  
3. Can you decompose the relative contributions of dataset diversity versus sheer scale once the similarity gap is matched?  
4. How did you select the cosine-similarity cutoff for pruning, and do results hold under different cutoff values?  
5. What is the wall-clock time and computational footprint for the full similarity-gap pruning pipeline?  

Rating  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold. The paper offers a novel and rigorous empirical contribution that advances our understanding of foundation-model generalization. Addressing the points above—especially expanding beyond ImageNet and reporting variance estimates—would further solidify its impact.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates whether CLIP’s remarkable out-of-distribution (OOD) and zero-shot performance arises merely from high train–test image similarity in its large-scale LAION-400M training set. Building on a perceptual similarity metric in CLIP’s embedding space, the authors first demonstrate a strong correlation between nearest-neighbor similarity and classification accuracy on six ImageNet variants. They then formalize a “similarity gap” between LAION and ImageNet-Train, surgically prune highly similar LAION samples to match this gap, and retrain CLIP on the resulting subsets. Surprisingly, even after removing all images more similar to the OOD test sets than any ImageNet-Train example, CLIP maintains most of its performance. Finally, the work identifies a core 100 M-image subset that preserves full OOD generalization, suggesting that factors beyond direct train–test overlap—such as scale and diversity—drive CLIP’s robustness.

Strengths:
1. Novel intervention. The similarity-gap formalism and surgical pruning are creative and go beyond prior robustness analyses, isolating the effect of near-duplicate training samples without changing model architecture or loss.
2. Rigorous, large-scale experiments. Retraining CLIP on multiple pruned splits across six benchmarks, with clear ablations, yields compelling evidence that removing highly similar samples only marginally degrades OOD performance.
3. Clear contributions. The discovery of a 100 M-image “core” LAION subset has practical implications for more efficient data curation and advances our understanding of the roles of scale and diversity in foundation-model generalization.
4. Well-structured presentation. Definitions of the similarity gap, pruning protocols, and key figures effectively illustrate the methodology and findings. The extensive appendix aids reproducibility.

Weaknesses:
1. Limited domain scope. The focus on ImageNet-based classification benchmarks leaves out other crucial CLIP applications such as retrieval, segmentation, or diverse tasks in VTAB. Experiments on MSCOCO→Flickr30k retrieval or additional VTAB splits would strengthen the generality of the conclusions.
2. Dependence on a single similarity metric. All analyses rely on CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to measure similarity. Alternative metrics—e.g., self-supervised ResNet features or text-based caption similarity—could reveal other modes of overlap.
3. Missing or unclear experimental details. It is not always clear how nearest-neighbor sets are constructed (how many neighbors, similarity thresholds), and confidence intervals or variance estimates over retraining runs are not reported, making it hard to assess statistical significance.
4. Comparison with ImageNet-Train distributions. While the paper aligns the closest-sample distances, it does not compare the full similarity distributions between the filtered LAION splits and ImageNet-Train, leaving open whether LAION still retains more “moderately similar” images.

Questions for the Authors:
• How sensitive are your findings to the choice of similarity threshold and embedding model?  
• Have you evaluated whether similar pruning interventions hold for other vision-language models (e.g., ALIGN, BASIC) or on non-ImageNet domains?  
• Can you provide error bars or confidence intervals to quantify variability across independent retraining runs?  
• What are the computational costs and implementation considerations for scaling similarity-gap pruning to multi-hundred-million image datasets?  

Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold. The paper makes a meaningful analytical contribution to understanding CLIP’s generalization, despite some limitations in scope and methodological details.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.
reviews.strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"
reviews.questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper tackles the important question of whether CLIP’s remarkable out-of-distribution (OOD) and zero-shot performance derives simply from the presence of near-duplicate or highly similar images in its massive LAION training set. To probe this, the authors introduce a perceptual similarity metric in CLIP’s embedding space, demonstrate a strong correlation between nearest-neighbor similarity and classification accuracy on six ImageNet-derived OOD benchmarks, and formalize a “similarity-gap” framework that surgical­ly prunes LAION to match ImageNet-Train’s train–test similarity distribution. After retraining CLIP on these pruned subsets — and even on a distilled “core” 100 M-image subset that preserves full performance — they observe only marginal drops in OOD accuracy. These results convincingly show that high train–test similarity alone cannot account for CLIP’s OOD generalization, pointing instead to dataset scale and diversity as primary drivers. Still, fully explaining CLIP’s robustness remains an open challenge.

Strengths:
- Novel Intervention: The similarity-gap formalism and targeted pruning represent a creative, principled method to isolate the effect of train–test overlap, going well beyond prior robustness analyses that focused on model or algorithmic changes.
- Rigorous Experiments: Large-scale retraining on multiple pruned splits across six benchmarks, combined with clear ablations (near- vs. far-pruning) and extensive visualizations, lend strong empirical support to the central claim.
- Core Subset Discovery: Identifying a 100 M-image LAION subset that matches full OOD performance is a valuable community resource for future studies and practical data curation.
- Resource Release: The code and pruned datasets are made publicly available, facilitating reproducibility and follow-up work.
- Clarity & Organization: Key concepts such as the similarity gap are clearly defined, figures and pseudocode aid comprehension, and thorough appendices document implementation details and hyperparameters.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Fairness of OOD Evaluation:  
   – The main review rightly points out that true OOD implies removal of an entire domain, not just images similar to each test sample. For instance, sketch-style images beyond those nearest to ImageNet-Sketch samples remain in the pruned training set, potentially bridging the domain gap. To strengthen the OOD claim, the authors should consider interventions that remove all images from a given domain (e.g., all sketches) rather than only their nearest neighbors.  
2. Reliance on a Single Similarity Metric:  
   – Current pruning depends exclusively on CLIP ViT-B embeddings. As several reviewers note, this may bias findings toward CLIP’s own representation. Evaluating alternative perceptual metrics (e.g., self-supervised ResNet features or larger CLIP variants) would clarify whether results hold under different notions of similarity.  
3. Sensitivity to Thresholds and Pruning Criteria:  
   – The choice of similarity thresholds and the pruning order can materially influence which samples are removed. A sensitivity analysis — varying cosine cutoffs or modifying the match-to-ImageNet criterion — would establish the robustness of performance drops.  
4. Statistical Significance & Variance Estimates:  
   – Retraining large models can incur run-to-run variability. Reporting confidence intervals or error bars over multiple random seeds would quantify the statistical significance of the observed performance differences.  
5. Limited Scope of Benchmarks:  
   – While ImageNet-based OOD sets are a natural starting point, extending the similarity-gap intervention to non-ImageNet domains (e.g., COCO detection, retrieval, or segmentation tasks) and other foundation models (e.g., ALIGN, BASIC) would enhance the generality of the conclusions.  
6. Caption Inclusion Rationale:  
   – Some pruned LAION splits augment images with ImageNet-Train captions. The motivation and impact of this caption augmentation should be clarified, and performance without caption inclusion should be evaluated.  
7. Computational Cost & Reproducibility:  
   – Measuring pairwise similarities at web scale and pruning billions of images is computationally intensive. Providing more detail on resource requirements, time per pruning run, and parallelization strategies would benefit practitioners seeking to replicate or extend this work.  
8. Terminology and Presentation:  
   – The term “generalization gap” is overloaded in the literature; adopting a more specific label (e.g., “similarity gap”) would reduce confusion. The abstract and contributions list could also be streamlined into a concise paragraph highlighting the main takeaway.

Minor Comments:
- Typo in Fig. 1 caption: “nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and ImageNet” should specify “ImageNet-Train” or clarify the intended dataset.
- Consider consolidating some appendix material (e.g., duplicate detection details) into the main text for clarity.
- Ensure consistency in naming pruned splits (e.g., “L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned)”).

Questions for the Authors:
1. How would performance change if you remove all images of a given test domain (e.g., sketches) rather than only the nearest neighbors?  
2. Have you tested similarity-gap pruning using alternative embedding backbones, and if so, with what outcome?  
3. Can you provide variance estimates across multiple retraining runs to assess the reliability of the observed performance drops?  
4. What guided the inclusion of ImageNet-Train captions in pruned LAION splits, and how critical is this augmentation for zero-shot performance?  
5. Could you extend the pruning methodology to other modalities or tasks (e.g., retrieval, segmentation) to demonstrate broader applicability?  

Rating:
6 – Marginally above the acceptance threshold. The paper poses a novel, well-executed empirical study that challenges a common assumption about train–test overlap and significantly contributes to our understanding of foundation-model generalization. Addressing the above weaknesses—particularly those concerning true OOD domain removal, sensitivity analyses, and statistical robustness—would further strengthen the work.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper tackles the question of whether CLIP’s remarkable zero‐shot and out‐of‐distribution (OOD) performance on ImageNet variants is primarily due to high train–test image similarity in its massive LAION‐400M pretraining set. The authors define a perceptual similarity metric in CLIP’s embedding space, show a strong positive correlation between nearest‐neighbor similarity and classification accuracy (Figures 1 & 3), and then surgically prune LAION—first by removing “near” versus “far” samples and then by enforcing a “similarity gap” that matches LAION’s similarity distribution to that of the much smaller ImageNet‐Train set. Large‐scale retraining on these pruned subsets yields only marginal drops in OOD accuracy, and the authors further isolate a 100 M‐image “core” subset that sustains full performance.  

Strengths:  
• Novel Framework: The similarity‐gap formalism and controlled pruning intervention provide a principled way to isolate the effect of highly similar training samples on OOD generalization—an angle not explored in prior robustness analyses.  
• Rigorous Large‐Scale Experiments: Retraining on multiple pruned splits across six ImageNet OOD benchmarks is technically demanding and shows that removing near‐duplicates alone cannot explain CLIP’s robustness.  
• Clear Empirical Findings: Figures 1 and 3 compellingly demonstrate that LAION contains many more semantically and stylistically similar neighbors to OOD test sets than ImageNet‐Train, and Figure 4 shows that these neighbors indeed boost accuracy.  
• Community Resource: Identifying a compact 100 M‐image core subset that preserves OOD performance offers practical guidance for future dataset curation and scaling studies.  
• Well‐Organized Presentation: Definitions, pseudocode for similarity‐gap pruning, and extensive visualizations make the methodology accessible, and the appendix documents key hyperparameters.

Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement:  
1. Scope of OOD Impact: While Figure 4 shows substantial accuracy drops when pruning near‐duplicates for ImageNet‐Sketch and ImageNet‐Val, it remains unclear whether those pruned samples are uniquely crucial for a single benchmark or support general classification. Plotting performance on both ImageNet‐Val and ImageNet‐Sketch after pruning Sketch‐similar images would clarify whether the effect is benchmark‐specific (Main Review).  
2. Comparison to Standard Models: The paper lacks a direct comparison with classifiers trained solely on ImageNet. Applying the same pruning methodology to the ImageNet‐only dataset would reveal whether CLIP loses OOD generalization more rapidly than standard models, helping assess if the observed drop is a general dataset‐scale phenomenon or specific to CLIP (Main Review).  
3. Reliance on Single Embedding Metric: All similarity measurements use CLIP ViT‐B embeddings. It would strengthen the claims to test alternative similarity spaces—e.g., self‐supervised ResNet features or text‐based caption similarity—to rule out embedding‐specific artifacts (Alternative Reviews 1, 3, 4).  
4. Sensitivity and Statistical Significance: The choice of similarity thresholds and pruning order can affect results, yet no ablation on threshold sensitivity or confidence intervals across multiple retraining runs is provided. Adding error bars and reporting variance would bolster the robustness of the conclusions (Alternative Review 2).  
5. Limited Generality Beyond ImageNet: The study focuses on ImageNet and its variants. Extending the similarity‐gap analysis to non‐ImageNet tasks (e.g., COCO segmentation or retrieval benchmarks) and other foundation models (ALIGN, BASIC) would demonstrate broader applicability (Alternative Reviews 2, 4).  
6. Reproducibility and Costs: Measuring and pruning billions of image pairs is computationally intensive. A clearer account of implementation details, compute resources, and run‐time statistics would aid reproducibility (Alternative Review 3).  
7. Theoretical and Conceptual Discussion: The manuscript would benefit from a deeper discussion of what “zero‐shot” and “OOD robustness” truly entail, and what it implies if models indeed “cheat” by memorizing near‐duplicates (Main Review).  

Minor Comments:  
– Page 7, typo: “Let us for consider” → “Let us consider.”  
– Section 5’s objective to “correct for highly similar images” needs clearer motivation: if pruning worsens benchmarks, why adopt this approach in practice?  

Questions for the Authors:  
1. How do results change with different similarity thresholds or alternative embedding models?  
2. Would pruning ImageNet alone reveal similar OOD performance drops for standard classifiers?  
3. Can you quantify performance variance across multiple retraining runs (e.g., error bars)?  
4. What was the rationale for including ImageNet‐Train captions in pruned LAION splits, and did you evaluate splits without this augmentation?  
5. How might transductive concerns and dataset licenses (e.g., ObjectNet’s no‐tuning clause) impact the legality or ethics of similarity‐based pruning?  

Rating: 5 – marginally below the acceptance threshold  
Despite its methodological novelty and extensive empirical evidence, the paper’s conclusions are presently constrained by reliance on a single similarity metric, absence of broader model and dataset comparisons, and insufficient statistical analysis. Addressing these limitations would significantly strengthen the work’s impact.

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper introduces Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a two-stage diffusion-based planner that decomposes long-horizon trajectory planning into a high-level “Sparse Diffuser” for generating subsampled subgoals and a low-level Diffuser for refining each segment into state–action sequences. By splitting offline trajectories into equal-length intervals (every Kth state) and parallelizing segment planning, HD achieves significant gains in return, planning speed, and out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization on Maze2D, AntMaze, Gym-MuJoCo, and FrankaKitchen benchmarks. A theoretical generalization bound characterizes the trade-off between subgoal sparsity (jump size K) and diffusion kernel receptive field. Compared to the flat Diffuser [1] and prior hierarchical methods, HD attains higher performance (e.g., +10–12% on Maze2D, +9% on MuJoCo), up to 10× speedups in planning time, and improved robustness to compositional OOD tasks.  

Strengths  
1. Hierarchical decomposition with diffusion.  Splitting planning into sparse subgoal generation and parallel low-level refinement is a conceptually simple yet effective extension of Diffuser, allowing long-horizon tasks to be solved with reduced computational cost. Several alternative reviews confirm that this drill-down strategy yields consistent empirical gains across a wide range of benchmarks.  
2. Empirical performance and efficiency.  HD outperforms the flat Diffuser and state-of-the-art offline RL and hierarchical planners on diverse tasks, including sparse-reward navigation and dense-reward control. The authors demonstrate 10× wall-clock speed improvements at inference, confirming the advantage of shorter high-level sequences and parallel low-level planning.  
3. Out-of-distribution generalization.  Through compositional tasks, HD shows robustness where flat planners fail, aligning with findings in alternative reviews that hierarchical abstractions facilitate better OOD behavior.  
4. Thorough ablations and analysis.  The paper includes ablations on jump size K, diffusion kernel size, inclusion of dense actions in the high level (HD-DA), and guidance variants. A theoretical bound links K and kernel size to generalization error, providing insight into design trade-offs.  

Weaknesses and Limitations  
1. Limited methodological novelty.  As noted in the main review, pairing two diffusion models for subgoals and segments is a pragmatic refinement of Diffuser rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. Similar subgoal abstractions exist in hierarchical RL, and related state-only diffusion methods (e.g., Decision-Diffuser [2]) suggest alternative design points.  
2. Fixed subgoal interval K.  All experiments use a pre-specified, uniform jump size K. While sensitivity analyses show trends, relying on a fixed interval may miss semantically meaningful waypoints. Alternative reviews suggest exploring adaptive or learned subgoal strategies to improve both performance and OOD generalization.  
3. Lack of feedback between levels.  The high-level diffuser samples subgoals independently of eventual low-level outcomes, raising concerns about non-optimal or self-overlapping paths. The paper does not detail how it prevents segment overlaps, nor does it investigate mechanisms for high-level adjustment based on low-level failure, as highlighted in the main review.  
4. Theory based on idealized assumptions.  The provided generalization bound offers intuition but depends on simplified assumptions (e.g., bounded network parameters, fixed K). As alternative reviews observe, the bounds remain somewhat abstract without empirical validation of the theoretical trade-offs.  
5. Reproducibility and implementation details.  Key choices—such as the number of trajectories sampled per subgoal segment, the guidance network’s calibration, and whether diffusion-sampled actions or an external controller are used for Maze2D—are deferred to appendices. This makes end-to-end reproduction challenging.  

Detailed Comments and Questions  
1. Sensitivity to jump size K  
   - Alternative reviews thoroughly query how performance varies with K. Can the authors provide systematic sensitivity curves across tasks and guidelines for selecting K? Would adaptive or value-based subgoal intervals yield better returns?  
2. Subgoal sampling and overlap  
   - How many candidate subgoal trajectories are sampled at high level, and how is overlap among segments prevented? Is there a validation or re-sampling step if low-level actions fail to reach a subgoal exactly?  
3. State-only diffusion ablation  
   - Given related work on state-only planning (Decision-Diffuser), an ablation comparing state-only versus state–action diffusion for subgoal and segment models would clarify the necessity of action conditioning at each level.  
4. Guidance network calibration  
   - The theoretical and empirical benefits of including dense actions in the high-level guidance network (HD-DA) are noted. What is the calibration error of the return predictor on subsampled trajectories, and how sensitive is planning quality to this error?  
5. Robustness to dataset coverage and noise  
   - How does HD perform when offline datasets are limited, biased, or noisy? Are there failure modes when subgoal diversity in data is low?  
6. Extension to high-dimensional observations  
   - Alternative reviews suggest testing on visual or partially observed domains. What modifications would be needed for pixel-based state representations or online world-model integration?  

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Hierarchical Diffuser is a well-engineered, practically significant extension of diffusion-based planning that demonstrably improves performance, efficiency, and OOD generalization across multiple challenging benchmarks. However, the core idea—a two-level diffusion planner with fixed subgoal intervals—represents an incremental rather than transformative advance over prior diffusion and hierarchical RL methods. The theoretical analysis, while insightful, relies on idealized assumptions and would benefit from tighter empirical validation. Given these considerations, I judge the work to be marginally above the acceptance threshold. It presents strong empirical contributions and opens useful directions for hierarchical diffusion planning, but would be strengthened by further ablations on subgoal adaptivity, state-only diffusion, and level-to-level feedback mechanisms.  

Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.

Strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.

Weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.

Questions:
See weakness above.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper presents Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a two-level planning framework that augments the flat Diffuser algorithm with temporal abstraction. Offline trajectories are split into coarse “jumpy” subgoals (every Kth state) handled by a high-level Sparse Diffuser, and dense segments between subgoals handled by a low-level Diffuser. At test time, HD first generates a sequence of subsampled states via guided diffusion and then refines each interval—parallelizing low-level planning for efficiency. Empirical results on long-horizon sparse-reward tasks (Maze2D, AntMaze), standard offline RL benchmarks (Gym-MuJoCo, FrankaKitchen), and compositional OOD tasks show that HD consistently outperforms flat Diffuser and prior hierarchical methods in return, planning speed (up to 10×), and OOD generalization. The authors further study the impact of diffusion kernel size and jumpy step size on data coverage, providing the interesting observation that small receptive fields fail to cover the full data distribution. A theoretical generalization bound characterizes the trade-off between subgoal sparsity (K) and kernel size.

Strengths:  
1. Clarity and Writing: The paper is generally well written, with clear algorithm pseudocode, illustrative diagrams of the HD pipeline, and structured presentation of experiments.  
2. Empirical Performance: HD yields strong gains in long-horizon planning, notably on AntMaze and Maze2D (+12% in Maze2D in some ablations), and shows improved returns on Gym-MuJoCo and FrankaKitchen. The method also demonstrates significant wall-clock speedups (up to 10×) over flat Diffuser.  
3. Thorough Evaluation: Experiments span sparse- and dense-reward tasks, standard offline RL benchmarks, compositional OOD tasks, and ablations on jump size K, kernel size, inclusion of dense actions, and guidance variants. These analyses build confidence in the method’s robustness.  
4. Theoretical Insight: A generalization bound relates subgoal interval and kernel size, offering insight into how temporal abstraction affects diffusion-model coverage and planning performance.  
5. Simplicity and Practicality: The fixed-interval hierarchical decomposition is straightforward to implement and parallelize, making HD appealing for practitioners aiming to scale diffusion planners to long horizons.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. Missing Baseline Comparison: The Decision Diffuser [Ajay et al., ICLR 2023], which employs a transformer architecture for conditional generative planning, is absent. Adding this baseline would clarify whether HD’s gains stem from hierarchy or architectural differences.  
2. Fixed Subgoal Interval (K): All alternative reviews note that reliance on a fixed K may limit adaptability. The manuscript would benefit from discussions or experiments on adaptive or learned subgoal intervals—perhaps value-based or learned waypoints—and guidelines for choosing K across tasks.  
3. Architectural Variants: The study focuses on a CNN backbone as in Diffuser. A comparison with transformer-based models (e.g., Decision Diffuser) could reveal the broader applicability of hierarchical diffusion.  
4. Theoretical Presentation: The generalization analysis, while valuable, uses dense notation and idealized assumptions (e.g., bounded network parameters). Simplifying the exposition or moving detailed proofs to the appendix could improve readability without losing rigor.  
5. Reproducibility Details: Key hyperparameters (e.g., kernel size, guidance strength) and algorithmic choices are partly buried in the appendix. Bringing essential settings into the main text would aid reproducibility.  
6. Guidance Network Calibration: Alternative reviews raise the question of how guidance-network errors affect planning. Empirical evaluation of its calibration and robustness to noisy return predictions would strengthen claims about guided diffusion.  
7. Dataset Coverage and Robustness: While HD shows strong OOD generalization on compositional tasks, its performance under limited or biased offline datasets (low coverage) remains unclear. Reporting results on smaller or skewed datasets would highlight failure modes.  
8. Extension to High-Dimensional Observations: The paper does not evaluate HD on image-based or partially observed settings. Even a discussion of required modifications (e.g., latent diffusion) would help readers assess applicability to vision-based domains.

Questions for the Authors:  
1. In Fig. 3, Diffuser with a small kernel size covers only one mode of the data distribution during execution. Is the standard Diffuser capable of covering the full distribution in principle but unable to do so at rollout time? What factors contribute to this gap?  
2. Also in Fig. 3, HD fails to cover two short trajectory branches despite high overall coverage. Can you explain why these segments are missed?  
3. How sensitive is HD’s performance to the choice of jump size K across different benchmarks? Do you have empirical guidelines for selecting K, or plans to learn it adaptively?  
4. Have you measured the calibration error of the guidance function on subsampled trajectories? How do guidance inaccuracies impact planning quality?  
5. Can you comment on HD’s behavior when the offline dataset is severely limited or biased? Are there failure cases when subgoal diversity is low?  
6. What would be required to extend HD to high-dimensional or visual observation spaces (e.g., pixel inputs)? Have you explored latent diffusion variants or learned world models in an online setting?

Rating: 6 – marginally above acceptance threshold.  
While Hierarchical Diffuser is a well-motivated and thoroughly evaluated approach that brings clear performance, efficiency, and OOD generalization gains, enhancements in baseline comparisons, adaptability of subgoal intervals, and deeper analysis of guidance robustness would further strengthen the contribution.
