reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper tackles unsupervised node‐level anomaly detection on graphs by addressing the inherent conflict between local inconsistency mining (LIM) and GNN message passing. Building on the observation that standard message passing can dilute local anomaly signals, the authors propose a two‐stage framework, GADAM. In Stage 1, an MLP‐based contrastive learning module computes local anomaly scores without aggregating neighborhood information. In Stage 2, these scores yield high‐confidence pseudo‐labels for a semi‐supervised objective that re‐introduces message passing via a hybrid attention mechanism—weighting neighbors according to anomaly‐score deviations and feature similarities—and applies a global consistency loss. Local and global scores are then combined to produce final anomaly rankings. The method is evaluated on nine benchmarks (including two large OGB graphs and both synthetic and organic anomalies), and compared against a wide range of baselines. GADAM demonstrates strong ROC‐AUC and AP improvements, faster runtime, and reduced GPU memory usage.

Strengths:
1. The core idea of decoupling LIM from message passing and subsequently reintroducing adaptive aggregation via hybrid attention is novel and well motivated. It directly addresses the smoothing dilemma in GNN‐based anomaly detection.
2. The two‐stage design—sampling‐free MLP for local scores plus pseudo‐label‐driven GNN refinement—offers a clear conceptual separation that yields measurable gains.
3. Experimental evaluation is extensive: nine datasets (seven injected‐anomaly and two real-anomaly), multiple metrics (ROC‐AUC, AP, Recall@k), and comparisons to ten recent baselines. Scalability tests on OGB graphs further demonstrate practical applicability.
4. The paper includes theoretical analysis of the global stage’s consistency loss, a complexity evaluation, and ablation studies for key components.
5. Runtime and memory benchmarks highlight substantial efficiency improvements over prior methods.

Weaknesses:
1. Problem formulation on structural anomalies remains unclear. The definition “structural anomalies are densely connected nodes” overlooks naturally dense networks and invites the question: why not simply use degree centrality? A baseline on node degree, or a discussion of when structural anomalies deviate from basic centrality measures, is missing.
2. The mechanism for capturing structural anomalies is not well justified. While local scores s^local (Eq. 6) naturally target contextual outliers, it is unclear how structural anomalies survive the L2 normalization in Eq. 3 or are subsequently amplified by hybrid attention.
3. The distinction between H_local and H_global is under‐explained, as both rely on the same normalization (Eq. 3). The rationale for labeling one representation “global” needs elaboration, possibly by clarifying how the global consistency loss (and attention scheduling) alters its semantics.
4. Key hyperparameters—pseudo‐label thresholds (kano, knor), the β scheduling strategy for pre‐ versus post‐attention weighting, and the hybrid attention coefficients—are selected without clear guidelines. Sensitivity analyses or automated threshold selection strategies would strengthen robustness claims.
5. The presentation is dense in places. Notation in the hybrid attention derivation can be hard to follow, and many implementation details (e.g., negative attention coefficients, β schedule) are deferred to the appendix, hindering reproducibility for a non‐expert reader.
6. Evaluation is still dominated by synthetic anomalies. Results on truly organic structural anomalies (e.g., intrusion detection, social‐network fraud) are limited to two datasets. Broader tests on diverse real‐world scenarios would bolster the paper’s practical significance.
7. The current results report overall performance, but separate metrics for contextual versus structural anomalies are absent. Such breakdowns, along with analysis of combined anomalies (nodes that are both contextual and structural), would provide deeper insight into GADAM’s strengths and failure modes.
8. The theoretical guarantee for the global stage (Appendix F) assumes ideal conditions (e.g., qTh+ = 1). Discussion of how deviations from these assumptions affect convergence or loss‐bound tightness is needed.
9. It remains unclear how pseudo‐label noise impacts the second stage. An ablation or discussion on the method’s resilience to noisy or imbalanced pseudo‐labels would be valuable.

Recommendations for Revision:
• Include degree centrality as a baseline and discuss cases where GADAM’s complexity is justified over simple heuristics.  
• Clarify the capture of structural anomalies: detail whether hybrid attention inherently emphasizes high‐degree nodes or if additional mechanisms are needed.  
• Elaborate the difference between H_local and H_global, and provide intuitive explanations (or visual aids) for the “diminishing pre‐attention vs. increasing post‐attention” claim.  
• Conduct sensitivity studies for kano/knor thresholds and β scheduling. Consider an automated threshold‐selection approach or guidelines for practitioners.  
• Decompose results by anomaly type (contextual, structural, and combined) and expand evaluation on purely organic anomaly benchmarks.  
• Provide more precise notation in the main text, bring key hyperparameter settings forward from the appendix, and detail implementation of negative attention coefficients.  
• Discuss the global consistency loss bound under realistic pseudo‐label noise and report any observed failure cases when pseudo‐label quality degrades.

Decision:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.  
While GADAM presents a compelling two‐stage strategy and achieves strong empirical gains, the issues around structural anomaly definition, hyperparameter sensitivity, clarity of method components, and reliance on synthetic datasets mean that the paper requires major revision before it can be recommended for acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper addresses the problem of unsupervised node-level anomaly detection in a graph through an adaptive message passing framework. The paper rightly motivates the fact that message passing in GNNs results in local anomaly signal loss. The authors have proposed an approach to obtain local and global anomaly scores through MLP and hybrid attention based adaptive message passing. The paper has conducted experiments on anomaly detection on real world datasets with both injected and ground truth anomalies, and used a good number of baseline approaches from the literature.

Strengths:
1. The idea of using local anomaly score to compute the global anomaly score is interesting.
2. The paper uses a good number of baseline algorithms from the literature.

Weaknesses:
1. The problem formulation seems to be problematic. Authors have mentioned "Structural anomalies are densely connected nodes in contrast to sparsely connected regular nodes". What about the networks that are dense by nature? Moreover, if density is the only criteria for being a structural anomaly, why can't we use degree of a node as the metric to find the structural anomalies? Are we over-complicating the solution of a relatively simple problem?

2. I am not convinced with the proposed solution. s^local (in Eq. 6) can only capture contextual anomalies, but not the structural anomalies because of the L2 normalization in Eq. 3. Where exactly are we capturing the structural anomalies in this framework?

3. What is the difference between H_local and H_global? They seem to be computed in the same way through Eq. 3. Why are we calling the second one as "global"?

4. Some of the claims in the paper should have been explained more. Can you please elaborate the reason of "as the training advances, the influence of pre-attention gradually diminishes, while the opposite for post-attention".

5. In Table 1, overall anomaly detection performance is reported. I am curious to see the performance on contextual and structural anomalies separately.

6. As mentioned before, degree centrality seems to be a good metric to capture the structural anomalies. Can you please include this heuristic as a baseline to compare with?

7. How do you capture the nodes which are both contextual and structural anomalies? Also, will your approach be able to capture "Combined Outliers" (or combined anomalies) as introduced in "Outlier Aware Network Embedding for Attributed Networks" (AAAI-2019)?

Questions:
Please see the questions above.

***********************************

After Rebuttal: The authors have tried to address all the concerns I had. I am changing my score from 3 to 5. I believe the paper needs a major revision to incorporate the recommended changes and the new experiments. I don't mind if this paper gets accepted in ICLR 2024 with those changes.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Effective Structural Encodings via Local Curvature Profiles

A theoretical result (Theorem 1) is also established suggesting improved expressivity due to LCP. However the result is rather qualitative without a quantitative characterization of the extent to which the expressivity is improved. Thus the theoretical development is rather light.

Overall the paper is very well written and, for most parts, easy to read. 

The idea is sound and the experiments look convincing to this reviewer.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper proposes Local Curvature Profiles (LCP) as a structural encoding for message‐passing graph neural networks (MPGNNs). Each node is augmented with five summary statistics—min, max, mean, standard deviation, and median—of discrete Ricci curvature values computed over its incident edges. The authors establish in Theorem 1 that MPGNNs with LCP strictly exceed the expressive power of the 1‐WL test by distinguishing classic non‐isomorphic graph pairs (Rooke vs. Shrikhande). Empirically, they evaluate LCP-augmented GCN, GIN, and GAT models on multiple node‐classification (e.g., Cora, Citeseer) and graph‐classification (e.g., Enzymes, IMDB, MUTAG, Proteins) benchmarks. LCP consistently outperforms standard positional and structural encodings and complements global positional features, while also demonstrating advantages over curvature‐based graph rewiring in mitigating over‐smoothing and over‐squashing.

Strengths:
1. Novelty and Theoretical Contribution  
   - Introducing discrete Ricci curvature as a per-node encoding is a fresh idea that builds on geometric graph theory.  
   - Theorem 1 provides a clear proof—via distinguishing Rooke and Shrikhande graphs—that LCP strictly increases MPGNN expressivity beyond 1‐WL.  
   - Related work on curvature-based rewiring and positional encodings is comprehensively surveyed.

2. Empirical Rigor  
   - Experiments cover multiple architectures (GCN, GIN, GAT) and tasks, with 100 random trials and confidence intervals reported.  
   - LCP yields substantial gains over baselines (spectral, random‐walk, substructure counts) and further boosts performance when combined with global positional encodings (e.g., Laplacian eigenvectors).  
   - Ablation studies evaluate alternative curvature notions (Ollivier, Forman, augmented Forman) and LCP variants, revealing that Ollivier‐Ricci curvature typically achieves the best trade‐off between accuracy and computational overhead.

3. Clarity and Reproducibility  
   - The manuscript is generally well organized and clearly written, with definitions and algorithms described in the main text and detailed appendices for implementation.  
   - Public release of code and hyperparameter settings is noted, aiding reproducibility.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Theoretical Quantification  
   - While Theorem 1 qualitatively establishes expressivity gains, the lack of a quantitative measure of how much expressivity improves limits theoretical insight. A more granular analysis—perhaps relating curvature statistics to distinguishing power—would strengthen the theoretical contribution.

2. Scalability and Computational Cost  
   - Exact computation of Ollivier‐Ricci curvature can be expensive on large or dense graphs. The current experiments focus on small to medium benchmarks.  
   - It would be valuable to include wall‐clock runtime and memory benchmarks for curvature computation (exact vs. approximate Sinkhorn vs. Forman approximations) on larger real‐world graphs, for instance OGB or social/biological networks with hundreds of thousands of nodes.

3. Scope of Evaluation  
   - Experiments are limited to classification tasks on relatively small datasets. Extending evaluation to large-scale graph regression tasks or heterogeneous feature settings would better demonstrate LCP’s generality and robustness.  
   - The sensitivity of downstream accuracy to the choice and number of summary statistics in LCP, as well as to curvature‐approximation thresholds, warrants further study. A brief parametric sweep over these design choices could guide practitioners.

4. Interaction with Deep Architectures  
   - The paper argues that LCP helps mitigate over‐smoothing and over‐squashing, yet quantitative comparisons to rewiring methods in very deep GNNs (beyond 10 layers) are not provided.  
   - Profiling training convergence, stability, and possible overfitting when stacking LCP-augmented layers could clarify these dynamics.

5. Hyperparameter and Implementation Details  
   - Although most settings are described, details such as the effect of LCP dimensionality on model capacity, thresholds for curvature approximation, and selection of summary statistics could be more explicitly stated in the main text rather than solely in the appendix.

Decision:
The paper makes a solid and well-motivated contribution by integrating discrete Ricci curvature into MPGNNs, supported by a sound (albeit qualitative) theoretical result and convincing empirical gains. While concerns remain regarding scalability, runtime profiling, and deeper quantitative analyses, these do not overshadow the novelty and practical impact of the approach. I recommend marginal acceptance (Rating: 6), as the work is above the threshold and will likely inspire further exploration of geometric encodings in graph learning.

reviews.summary:
The paper addresses the crucial issue of improving the performance of GNNs through structural encodings. The authors present a novel approach based on discrete Ricci curvature, termed Local Curvature Profiles (LCP), and demonstrate its significant effectiveness in enhancing GNN performance. They also investigate the combination of local structural encodings with global positional encodings and compare these encoding types with curvature-based rewiring techniques. The paper makes important contributions to the field of Graph Machine Learning and provides valuable insights into the potential of curvature-based encodings.

==================================

Update: I appreciate the authors for answering my questions and providing more experimental results. I would like to keep my scores.
reviews.strengths:
- LCP provides a unique way to encode the geometry of a node's neighborhood, and the paper convincingly demonstrates its superior performance in node and graph classification tasks.

This finding is valuable as it suggests that using a combination of different encoding types can result in enhanced downstream performance. reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper tackles the important problem of enriching Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with structural encodings by introducing Local Curvature Profiles (LCP), a novel way to encode the discrete Ricci curvature of each node’s incident edges via five summary statistics (min, max, mean, standard deviation, median). Building on a solid theoretical foundation, the authors prove that appending LCP strictly increases the expressivity of message‐passing GNNs beyond the 1‐Weisfeiler–Lehman test—exemplified by distinguishing non‐isomorphic strongly regular graphs (Rooke vs. Shrikhande). Empirically, LCP is evaluated across multiple architectures (GCN, GIN, GAT) and tasks (node classification on Cora/Citeseer; graph classification on Enzymes, MUTAG, Proteins, IMDB), demonstrating consistent and often substantial improvements over existing structural and positional encodings as well as curvature‐based rewiring methods. Notably, combining LCP with global positional encodings yields further gains, underscoring their complementarity.

Strengths:
- Originality and Theoretical Soundness: LCP repurposes discrete Ricci curvature as a drop-in structural encoding. The expressivity proof is clear and leverages classic WL limitations.
- Empirical Rigor: Extensive experiments with 100 random trials, confidence intervals, and ablations on curvature notions (Ollivier, Forman, Augmented Forman) and LCP variants demonstrate that Ollivier–Ricci curvature yields the best trade-off between cost and performance.
- Complementarity of Encodings: The finding that local (LCP) and global (Laplacian eigenvectors, random walks) encodings capture different aspects of graph structure—and that their combination further boosts accuracy—is well supported.
- Practical Integration: LCP requires no changes to GNN layers and can be applied as a preprocessing step, making it easy to adopt in existing pipelines.
- Clarity of Presentation: Despite some dense sections, the manuscript is generally well organized, with informative figures, tables, and a comprehensive appendix.

Weaknesses:
- Readability: Certain parts of the introduction and the curvature background are dense; newcomers to discrete Ricci curvature may struggle. Clarifying notation and providing a more intuitive motivation up front would broaden accessibility.
- Dataset Diversity and Scalability: All benchmarks are relatively small. The paper lacks evaluation on large‐scale real‐world graphs (e.g., OGB, social/biological networks with hundreds of thousands of nodes). Exact Ollivier–Ricci curvature computation can become a bottleneck; while ablations consider faster curvature variants, wall-clock runtime and memory benchmarks on large graphs are missing.
- Reproducibility Details: Hyperparameter choices—such as curvature approximation thresholds, number of LCP bins, and the impact of LCP feature dimensionality on overfitting—are not fully specified. Clearer guidance on selecting summary statistics and curvature methods would aid practitioners.
- Interaction with Deep Architectures: It remains unclear how LCP affects training convergence, over‐smoothing, and over‐squashing in deeper GNNs (beyond ~10 layers), or when combined with attention mechanisms or richer node features (continuous or high-dimensional inputs).
- Applicability to Diverse Graph Types: Extensions to directed or weighted graphs are not addressed, nor is the behavior of LCP under heterogeneous feature settings.

Questions for the Authors:
1. How does the computational overhead of exact Ollivier–Ricci curvature scale on large real-world graphs, and can approximate or combinatorial curvature bounds achieve similar empirical gains?
2. How sensitive are downstream results to the choice and number of summary statistics in the LCP, and to the overall LCP feature dimensionality?
3. Have you profiled training convergence and stability when using LCP in deeper GNNs? Is there any degradation due to over‐smoothing or over‐squashing?
4. Can you provide more details on hyperparameter ranges for curvature approximations and how these affect both runtime and performance?
5. Have you considered applying LCP to directed or weighted graphs, or to datasets with continuous/high-dimensional node attributes?

Recommendation:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
This paper makes a significant contribution by combining a rigorous expressivity proof with strong empirical evidence that discrete Ricci curvature summaries enhance GNN performance. Addressing the noted clarity and scalability concerns would further strengthen the work, but in its current form it merits acceptance.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes improving graph neural networks, e.g. graph convnets. The idea is to encode structural information through Local Curvature Profiles, enabling each node to better characterize the geometry of its neighborhood. Instead of rewriting the graph, the proposed approach adds summary statistics about each node's local curvature to the features of each node.

On a variety of different tasks, this approach improves the performance of the resulting graph neural nets.
reviews.strengths:
This paper seems reasonable to this reviewer, outperforming baseline encoding approaches or approaches that require rewiring. 

Perhaps most surprising to this reviewer is that it improves performance of GATs (seemingly similar to transforms in that they use self-attention?) as it would seem reasonable that such a network would be able to dynamically compute something similar to these statistics.

The experiments seem reasonably done at least to this reviewer (not an expert in this area at all), involving both LCP itself as well as combining it with positional encoding, and then later rewiring.
reviews.weaknesses: It's not obvious to this reviewer what the weaknesses are. The main concern to this reviewer is that some large pretrained transformer could do better than any of the proposed methods, but that's a very general concern these days. Possibly this approach or GNNs in general could work better on more specialized tasks where there are a very large number of nodes.
reviews.questions: How do the results compare versus model size? E.g. could making a GCN deeper allow it to implicitly compute these kinds of features itself? What's stopping it from doing that?

Summary:
This paper proposes improving graph neural networks, e.g. graph convnets. The idea is to encode structural information through Local Curvature Profiles, enabling each node to better characterize the geometry of its neighborhood. Instead of rewriting the graph, the proposed approach adds summary statistics about each node's local curvature to the features of each node.

On a variety of different tasks, this approach improves the performance of the resulting graph neural nets.

Strengths:
This paper seems reasonable to this reviewer, outperforming baseline encoding approaches or approaches that require rewiring. 

Perhaps most surprising to this reviewer is that it improves performance of GATs (seemingly similar to transforms in that they use self-attention?) as it would seem reasonable that such a network would be able to dynamically compute something similar to these statistics.

The experiments seem reasonably done at least to this reviewer (not an expert in this area at all), involving both LCP itself as well as combining it with positional encoding, and then later rewiring.

Weaknesses:
It's not obvious to this reviewer what the weaknesses are. The main concern to this reviewer is that some large pretrained transformer could do better than any of the proposed methods, but that's a very general concern these days. Possibly this approach or GNNs in general could work better on more specialized tasks where there are a very large number of nodes.

Questions:

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces Local Curvature Profiles (LCP), a novel structural encoding for message-passing Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Rather than modifying the graph structure, LCP augments each node’s features with five simple summary statistics (min, max, mean, standard deviation, median) of the discrete Ricci curvature on its incident edges. The authors theoretically show that incorporating LCP strictly increases expressivity beyond the 1‐Weisfeiler-Leman test by distinguishing non‐isomorphic strongly regular graphs (e.g. Rooke vs. Shrikhande). Empirically, they evaluate LCP across GCN, GIN, and GAT on both node classification (Cora, Citeseer) and graph classification benchmarks (Enzymes, IMDB, MUTAG, Proteins, etc.). Results consistently demonstrate that LCP outperforms existing structural encodings (substructure counts), positional encodings (Laplacian eigenvectors, random walks), and curvature-based rewiring methods (e.g. BORF). Moreover, combining LCP with global positional encodings yields further gains, and ablation studies confirm that Ollivier-Ricci curvature provides the best trade-off between expressivity and computational cost compared to Forman or augmented Forman variants.

Strengths  
1. Novelty and Theoretical Contribution  
   • The use of discrete Ricci curvature as a per‐node summary encoding is a fresh, well-motivated idea drawing on geometric theory.  
   • The proof that LCP enhances expressivity beyond the 1-WL test is sound and leverages classic non-isomorphic graph pairs, providing solid theoretical grounding.  

2. Empirical Rigor  
   • The experimental evaluation is extensive: three GNN architectures, six graph and two node classification datasets, 100 random trials with confidence intervals.  
   • Ablation studies on curvature definitions (Ollivier, Forman, augmented Forman) and LCP variants (different summary statistics) underline design choices and robustness.  
   • LCP consistently yields substantial accuracy improvements—up to 20% on some graph classification tasks—over strong baselines and complements positional encodings seamlessly.  

3. Practicality and Clarity  
   • LCP is a straightforward drop-in preprocessing step that does not alter model architectures or training regimes.  
   • The paper is generally well written, with clear definitions, motivating examples, and an organized structure. Reproducibility is enhanced by sharing code and detailed appendices.  

Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. Scalability of Curvature Computation  
   • All experiments are conducted on mid-scale benchmarks. The wall-clock runtime, memory usage, and scalability of computing exact Ollivier-Ricci curvature on large real-world graphs (hundreds of thousands or millions of nodes/edges) are not fully evaluated.  
   • It would strengthen the paper to include profiling of exact vs. approximate curvature methods (e.g. Sinkhorn iterations, Forman bounds) and to report end-to-end overhead relative to training time.  

2. Sensitivity to LCP Design Choices  
   • While ablations cover different curvature notions, more discussion is needed on how sensitive downstream performance is to the choice and number of summary statistics or binning strategies in the LCP.  
   • Clarification on hyperparameter ranges (e.g. thresholds for curvature approximation, LCP feature dimensionality) would aid reproducibility and provide guidance for practitioners.  

3. Interaction with Model Capacity and Depth  
   • A question remains whether deeper GCNs or larger transformer-style GNNs could implicitly learn similar curvature features, potentially narrowing the gap. Comparative experiments varying model size or depth would help isolate LCP’s contribution.  
   • It would be useful to understand if LCP exacerbates or mitigates known issues like over-smoothing or over-squashing in deeper architectures.  

4. Broader Applicability  
   • The current focus is on homogeneous, undirected graphs with simple node attributes. It is unclear how LCP performs with high-dimensional or continuous features, or on heterogeneous or directed networks.  
   • Validation on large-scale benchmarks (e.g. OGB datasets, social or biological networks) would demonstrate generality and real-world applicability.  

Questions for the Authors  
1. Can you provide detailed runtime and memory benchmarks for computing exact and approximate curvature on larger graphs?  
2. How do the performance gains vary with model size/depth—could a deeper GCN or a transformer-based GNN match LCP-augmented shallow models?  
3. Which of the five summary statistics contribute most to downstream accuracy, and is there a minimal subset that retains most gains?  
4. Have you observed any stability or convergence differences when training LCP-augmented GNNs, especially in deeper settings?  
5. Could you comment on extending LCP to directed or weighted graphs, and whether the theoretical expressivity results carry over?  

Recommendation  
Rating: 8: accept, good paper  
This work presents a compelling combination of theoretical novelty, practical simplicity, and strong empirical gains. While questions about scalability and hyperparameter sensitivity remain, LCP represents a significant advance in structural encoding for GNNs. Addressing the outlined concerns would further strengthen an already solid contribution likely to influence future research in geometric graph representation learning.

reviews.summary: The paper explores the efficacy of various structural encodings, along with their integration with global positional encodings, to enhance the performance of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) in downstream tasks. It introduces a novel structural encoding known as LCP, derived from discrete Ricci curvature, which demonstrates superior performance compared to existing encoding methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. The article introduces LCP encoding which presents how curvature information may affect GNN performance, contributing to a better understanding of the research context and significance
2. The article conducts comprehensive experiments on various datasets; however, it could benefit from additional experiments to investigate the underlying reasons why LCP is effective
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The article lacks explanations for some crucial implementation steps, making it confusing to read. I suggest the author improve the presentation and logic of the article to enhance clarity (see question 1 and 2)
2. Please provide definitions for variable names, such as 'd_max.' Currently, there are many variables in the article that are not explained, which can be challenging for newcomers to understand
3. In spite of keeping settings and optimization hyperparameters consistent among different settings, the authors should still provide the corresponding parameter configurations. This would aid in experiment reproducibility and, as a result, make the results more robust.
4. In Section 3.1, the authors mention, 'We believe that the curvature information of edges away from the extremes of the curvature distribution, which is not being used by curvature-based rewiring methods, can be beneficial to GNN performance.' I consider this assertion somewhat speculative, and I did not find any subsequent experiments that substantiate this claim. Would it be possible to include relevant ablation experiments to support this hypothesis?
5. In Section 3.1, the authors define LCP as 'five summary statistics of the CMS.' I would appreciate a more detailed motivation for this particular definition. Additionally, it would be beneficial to include relevant ablation experiments that showcase the impact of removing specific summary statistics to demonstrate their significance in influencing the final results.

2. Could the authors please elaborate on how the combination of LCP encoding and position encoding is implemented in Section 4.2.2? I couldn't find any details regarding the actual implementation.

Summary:
The paper explores the efficacy of various structural encodings, along with their integration with global positional encodings, to enhance the performance of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) in downstream tasks. It introduces a novel structural encoding known as LCP, derived from discrete Ricci curvature, which demonstrates superior performance compared to existing encoding methods.

Strengths:
1. The article introduces LCP encoding which presents how curvature information may affect GNN performance, contributing to a better understanding of the research context and significance
2. The article conducts comprehensive experiments on various datasets; however, it could benefit from additional experiments to investigate the underlying reasons why LCP is effective

Weaknesses:
1. The article lacks explanations for some crucial implementation steps, making it confusing to read. I suggest the author improve the presentation and logic of the article to enhance clarity (see question 1 and 2)
2. Please provide definitions for variable names, such as 'd_max.' Currently, there are many variables in the article that are not explained, which can be challenging for newcomers to understand
3. In spite of keeping settings and optimization hyperparameters consistent among different settings, the authors should still provide the corresponding parameter configurations. This would aid in experiment reproducibility and, as a result, make the results more robust.
4. In Section 3.1, the authors mention, 'We believe that the curvature information of edges away from the extremes of the curvature distribution, which is not being used by curvature-based rewiring methods, can be beneficial to GNN performance.' I consider this assertion somewhat speculative, and I did not find any subsequent experiments that substantiate this claim. Would it be possible to include relevant ablation experiments to support this hypothesis?

Additionally, it would be beneficial to include relevant ablation experiments that showcase the impact of removing specific summary statistics to demonstrate their significance in influencing the final results.

Questions:

2. Could the authors please elaborate on how the combination of LCP encoding and position encoding is implemented in Section 4.2.2? I couldn't find any details regarding the actual implementation.

3: reject, not good enough
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates the role of structural encodings in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) by introducing a novel Local Curvature Profile (LCP) based on discrete Ricci curvature. LCP augments each node with five summary statistics (min, max, mean, standard deviation, median) of the curvature of its incident edges, and the authors prove that appending LCP strictly increases GNN expressivity beyond the 1-Weisfeiler–Lehman test. Empirically, the method is evaluated on a suite of standard node and graph classification benchmarks (e.g., Cora, Citeseer, Enzymes, IMDB, MUTAG, Proteins) across multiple architectures (GCN, GIN, GAT), demonstrating consistent gains over existing positional (Laplacian eigenvectors, random‐walk features) and structural encodings (substructure counts), as well as curvature-based rewiring methods. The authors further show that combining LCP with global positional encodings yields additional improvements and include ablations comparing different curvature notions (Ollivier, Forman, augmented Forman) and LCP definitions.

Strengths:
1. Novelty and Theoretical Contribution: The use of discrete Ricci curvature as a generic node‐level structural encoding is original and well motivated by geometric theory. The proof that LCP enhancement increases message-passing GNN expressivity beyond 1-WL by distinguishing classical non‐isomorphic graphs is a substantive theoretical advance.
2. Empirical Performance: Across six graph and two node classification benchmarks, LCP-augmented models consistently outperform strong baselines, often by substantial margins, and complement global positional encodings when combined. These results are supported by multiple architectures, 100 randomized trials, and confidence intervals.
3. Thorough Ablations: The paper presents ablations over curvature definitions and alternative LCP constructions, shedding light on the trade-offs between computational cost and predictive performance. In particular, Ollivier–Ricci curvature is shown to deliver the best accuracy, while Forman-based approximations offer a lower‐cost alternative.
4. Clear Positioning: The manuscript situates LCP relative to both existing structural encodings and curvature-based graph rewiring approaches, demonstrating that direct encoding of curvature summaries yields superior mitigation of over-smoothing and over-squashing without altering the message-passing layers.

Weaknesses:
1. Presentation and Clarity Gaps: Critical implementation details are omitted or under-explained. For example, variable names such as ‘d_max’ are introduced without definition, and the “no encodings (NO)” baseline is not clearly described. Similarly, the procedure for integrating LCP with positional encodings in Section 4.2.2 lacks sufficient detail, making reproduction difficult.
2. Missing Hyperparameter and Experimental Configurations: Although the authors claim consistent optimization settings across experiments, the exact hyperparameter values (e.g., learning rates, number of layers, curvature approximation thresholds) are not provided in the main text or appendix. This omission hinders reproducibility and raises questions about the robustness of the reported gains.
3. Scalability and Runtime Concerns: While exact Ollivier–Ricci curvature underpins LCP’s performance, the computational overhead on large or dense graphs remains unquantified. The paper does not report wall-clock time or memory usage for curvature computation on real-world networks with hundreds of thousands to millions of edges, nor does it evaluate approximate or Sinkhorn-based curvature methods at scale.
4. Limited Benchmark Scope: All experiments are conducted on modest‐sized, well-studied datasets. It remains unclear whether LCP generalizes to larger, more heterogeneous graphs (e.g., OGB benchmarks, social or biological networks) or tasks beyond classification, such as graph regression.
5. Speculative Claims Without Supporting Ablations: The authors suggest that curvature information “away from the extremes of the distribution” is beneficial to GNN performance, yet no dedicated ablation isolates this effect. Likewise, the choice of exactly five summary statistics for LCP is asserted without a detailed motivation or controlled study demonstrating the contribution of each statistic to the final results.
6. Interaction with Model Capacity and Overfitting: Although LCP boosts accuracy, the impact of increasing feature dimensionality on overfitting and convergence stability—particularly in deeper GNNs—is not examined. Questions remain as to whether LCP’s benefits persist or degrade as model depth increases.

Suggestions for Improvement:
1. Enhance Clarity: Define all variables (e.g., d_max) at first use, fully describe the “NO encoding” baseline, and detail the implementation steps for combining LCP with positional encodings. A schematic outlining the data pipeline would be valuable.
2. Provide Reproducibility Details: Include a complete table of hyperparameter choices, optimization settings, and curvature approximation parameters. Sharing code is a good start, but the manuscript should stand alone in enabling replication.
3. Evaluate Scalability: Report runtime and memory benchmarks for exact and approximate curvature computations on larger real‐world graphs. Assess LCP’s performance and computational trade-offs on a large‐scale dataset (e.g., OGB).
4. Conduct Targeted Ablations: Design experiments to validate the claim about non-extreme curvature values, and perform leave-one-out studies on the five summary statistics to justify their selection.
5. Study Depth and Overfitting: Explore how LCP interacts with deeper GNN architectures, and analyze whether the increased feature dimension leads to overfitting or affects training stability.

Decision:
3: Reject – While the paper presents a novel and theoretically grounded approach with promising empirical gains, substantial gaps in clarity, reproducibility, scalability evaluations, and targeted ablations currently prevent full confidence in the method’s generality and implementation.

title: Principled Federated Domain Adaptation: Gradient Projection and Auto-Weighting

reviews.summary: This paper proposes two algorithms to solve federated domain adaptation, a case in which there exists a distributional/domain shift between clients in federated learning. The authors tackle the problems of domain shift and data scarcity in their work. To solve these problems, they propose to design algorithms concerning the __server aggregation rule__, i.e., how the server merges the different gradients of the same model coming from the clients. The two proposed methods are called __FedDA__ which does a convex combination of clients' gradients (including the target), and __FedGP__, which extracts information from source clients' gradients based on the target client gradients.
reviews.strengths:
__Originality.__ The authors provide a novel theoretical framework for the analysis of Federated Learning under heterogeneity.

__Quality.__ The paper is globally well-written and clear. Parts of the experimental section could be improved

__Clarity.__ The novel theoretical framework is easy to follow. Assumptions, notation and definitions are clearly stated and the proposed algorithms are intuitive.

__Significance.__ This paper tackles an extremly important problem in federated learning, i.e., how to deal with __client heterogeneity__. In this sense, besides being important for the niche of federated DA, it can also impact federated learning in general.
reviews.weaknesses:
__Major__

__W1.__ The description of the real-world experiments in the main paper is not sufficient. While the authors do provide enough information in the appendix, the main paper does not fully describes the methodology the authors employed in consolidating the results of Table 1. For instance, how are the labeled data points chosen for the experiments? How does the performance change w.r.t. to the choice of data points (i.e. standard deviation of the accuracy on target domain)? These questions are answered in the appendix, but they should be clear in the main paper.

__Minor.__ (note, this point __did not__ impacted negatively in my review).

__W2.__ I would like to raise that, while this is a Federated DA paper, the authors assume access to a (small) set of labeled data in the target domain. This somewhat breaks the rules of _Unsupervised_ DA, and may bias performance towards methods that use target labeled data when comparing with UDA algorithms such as KD3A. This remains somewhat true even when supposing a small amount of labeled target data, depending on the degree of distributional shift. Nonetheless, __I do think the authors use labeled data in a clearly motivated and justified way__.
reviews.questions: __Q1.__ Concerning __FedDA__ and __FedGP__ aggregation schemes, in order to have a convex combination, shouldn't $\sum_{i=1}^{n}\beta_{i}=1$? Is this constraint enforced? For instance, in Figure 9 (appendix), the sum of betas exceeds 1.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes two algorithms to solve federated domain adaptation, a case in which there exists a distributional/domain shift between clients in federated learning. The authors tackle the problems of domain shift and data scarcity in their work. To solve these problems, they propose to design algorithms concerning the __server aggregation rule__, i.e., how the server merges the different gradients of the same model coming from the clients. The two proposed methods are called __FedDA__ which does a convex combination of clients' gradients (including the target), and __FedGP__, which extracts information from source clients' gradients based on the target client gradients.

Strengths:
__Originality.__ The authors provide a novel theoretical framework for the analysis of Federated Learning under heterogeneity.

__Quality.__ The paper is globally well-written and clear. Parts of the experimental section could be improved

__Clarity.__ The novel theoretical framework is easy to follow. Assumptions, notation and definitions are clearly stated and the proposed algorithms are intuitive.

__Significance.__ This paper tackles an extremly important problem in federated learning, i.e., how to deal with __client heterogeneity__. In this sense, besides being important for the niche of federated DA, it can also impact federated learning in general.

Weaknesses:
__Major__

__W1.__ The description of the real-world experiments in the main paper is not sufficient. While the authors do provide enough information in the appendix, the main paper does not fully describes the methodology the authors employed in consolidating the results of Table 1. For instance, how are the labeled data points chosen for the experiments? How does the performance change w.r.t. to the choice of data points (i.e. standard deviation of the accuracy on target domain)? These questions are answered in the appendix, but they should be clear in the main paper.

__Minor.__ (note, this point __did not__ impacted negatively in my review).

__W2.__ I would like to raise that, while this is a Federated DA paper, the authors assume access to a (small) set of labeled data in the target domain. This somewhat breaks the rules of _Unsupervised_ DA, and may bias performance towards methods that use target labeled data when comparing with UDA algorithms such as KD3A. This remains somewhat true even when supposing a small amount of labeled target data, depending on the degree of distributional shift. Nonetheless, __I do think the authors use labeled data in a clearly motivated and justified way__.

Questions:
__Q1.__ Concerning __FedDA__ and __FedGP__ aggregation schemes, in order to have a convex combination, shouldn't $\sum_{i=1}^{n}\beta_{i}=1$? Is this constraint enforced? For instance, in Figure 9 (appendix), the sum of betas exceeds 1.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The submission studied domain adaptation under the federated setting. Two methods (FedDA (1) and FedGP(2)) to aggregate gradients were proposed based on the analysis of the delta error of an aggregation rule (Theorem 3.6). An auto-weighting rule (3) was proposed (FedDA_Auto and FedGP_Auto), too. Experiments showing the robustness (Figure 2) and target accuracies (Table 1) justified the proposed methods.
reviews.strengths:
The submission identifies the factors that affect the performance of an aggregation rule and then proposes solutions based on the findings.
The target domain enjoys the robustness and performance increases brought by the solutions.
reviews.weaknesses:
Despite a comfortable reading experience and leading performance results, I would like to raise a concern about the problem formulation.

(a) From the federated learning perspective, the server and the clients are learning together to achieve a better performance measured by the sum of ALL clients. Therefore, federatively speaking, paying the whole attention to ONE target client might not align with the original intention of studying federated learning.
reviews.questions:
(b) Given the federated learning nature, multiple target clients seem more practical. How would the proposed method scale with the number of target clients?

(c) The current source clients are given and assumed to be well-trained. What are the potential and challenges to extending the proposed method to a scenario where every client learns and transfers simultaneously?

(d) What if one negates the direction of the projections of negative source gradients (e.g., the projections of g_s3 and g_s4 in Figure 1)?

(e) The behaviors of FedDA in Figure 2(a) and Figure 2(c) seem contracdict to each other. Is it trivial? Or may I have a clarification?

Two methods (FedDA (1) and FedGP(2)) to aggregate gradients were proposed based on the analysis of the delta error of an aggregation rule (Theorem 3.6). An auto-weighting rule (3) was proposed (FedDA_Auto and FedGP_Auto), too. Experiments showing the robustness (Figure 2) and target accuracies (Table 1) justified the proposed methods.

Strengths:
The submission identifies the factors that affect the performance of an aggregation rule and then proposes solutions based on the findings.
The target domain enjoys the robustness and performance increases brought by the solutions.

Weaknesses:
Despite a comfortable reading experience and leading performance results, I would like to raise a concern about the problem formulation.

(a) From the federated learning perspective, the server and the clients are learning together to achieve a better performance measured by the sum of ALL clients. Therefore, federatively speaking, paying the whole attention to ONE target client might not align with the original intention of studying federated learning.

Questions:
(b) Given the federated learning nature, multiple target clients seem more practical. How would the proposed method scale with the number of target clients?

(c) The current source clients are given and assumed to be well-trained. What are the potential and challenges to extending the proposed method to a scenario where every client learns and transfers simultaneously?

(d) What if one negates the direction of the projections of negative source gradients (e.g., the projections of g_s3 and g_s4 in Figure 1)?

(e) The behaviors of FedDA in Figure 2(a) and Figure 2(c) seem contracdict to each other. Is it trivial? Or may I have a clarification?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper addresses federated domain adaptation (FDA), where multiple source clients with ample labeled data and a server collaborate to improve a single target client suffering from data scarcity and domain shift. The authors introduce a rigorous theoretical framework that defines a “Delta error,” decomposing the discrepancy between any aggregation rule and the true target gradient into a source–target domain distance term and a target gradient variance term (Theorem 3.6). On this basis, they propose two aggregation rules—FedDA (a convex combination of source and target gradients) and FedGP (which projects and filters source gradients along the target direction)—and derive closed‐form auto‐weighting schemes (FedDA_Auto and FedGP_Auto) that optimally balance bias and variance. Extensive experiments on semi‐synthetic data (ColoredMNIST) and real benchmarks (VLCS, TerraIncognita, DomainNet, PACS, Office-Home) demonstrate that the proposed methods achieve consistent robustness (Figure 2) and improved target accuracies (Table 1) compared to standard federated averaging, personalized FL, UFDA, and domain generalization baselines.

Strengths  
1. Principled Theoretical Framework: Building on a clean decomposition of the Delta error into domain‐distance and variance components, the paper offers rigorous convergence and generalization bounds. This stands out compared to prior work that often relies on heuristic aggregation.  
2. Novel Aggregation Rules: FedDA and FedGP are directly derived from theory, rather than ad-hoc design. In particular, the projection‐based FedGP effectively filters out conflicting source gradients, a clear advance for handling strong domain shifts.  
3. Auto‐Weighting Schemes: The closed‐form formulas for optimal weights provide practical server‐side algorithms (FedDA_Auto, FedGP_Auto) that require no changes to client code, making the methods lightweight to deploy.  
4. Empirical Validation: The experimental suite is extensive, covering both controlled synthetic shifts and a broad range of real datasets. The empirical match between predicted Delta error and observed performance (e.g., Figure 2) lends credibility to the theoretical analysis.  
5. Clarity of Presentation: The paper is generally well organized, with intuitive figures (e.g., Figure 1) illustrating gradient projection and conflict. Appendices supply detailed proofs and implementation notes, supporting reproducibility.

Weaknesses  
1. Federated Learning Formulation: From a federated learning perspective, focusing predominantly on one target client departs from the usual objective of optimizing the aggregate performance over all clients. It remains unclear whether emphasizing a single target aligns with broader cross-silo FL goals.  
2. Multi-Target Scalability: The paper assumes a single target client. In practice, supporting multiple target clients concurrently would be more realistic. How would the auto-weighting and projection rules scale as the number of targets grows?  
3. Theoretical Approximations: The derivation of FedGP’s Delta error relies on mean-field and high-dimensional approximations (e.g., τ≈const, δ≈1). The practical accuracy of these approximations is not empirically quantified. More precise bounds or empirical studies of the approximation error would strengthen the theoretical claims.  
4. Computational and Communication Overhead: While the auto-weighting is server‐side, it requires the server to receive multiple gradient batches from the target client. The overhead in communication, storage, and computation—particularly with many source clients or large B—deserves more thorough analysis.  
5. Choice of Empirical Distribution π and Batch Size B: The optimal weights depend on estimates of source–target distance and target variance via a distribution π over parameters and batch size B. The sensitivity of deployment to these hyperparameters is not fully explored; practical guidelines would help practitioners.  
6. Presentation Density: Some parts of the exposition, especially the derivations of the auto-weight estimators, are heavy with notation and deferred to the appendix. A high‐level schematic or simplified summary in the main text could aid reader comprehension.  
7. Privacy Implications: Sending multiple gradient updates from the target client to the server for weight estimation may raise privacy concerns. A brief discussion of privacy trade‐offs or mitigation strategies would be valuable.

Questions for the Authors  
1. Multi-Target Extension: Can your framework be extended to handle multiple target clients simultaneously? What changes to the aggregation and auto-weighting rules would be required?  
2. Scaling to Many Clients: How does communication and computation overhead scale when the number of source clients and gradient batches B grows large? Are there lightweight approximations or sampling schemes you recommend?  
3. Accuracy of Approximations: Have you measured the gap between the approximate optimal weights (under mean-field/high-dim assumptions) and those obtained via full computation?  
4. Hyperparameter Sensitivity: How sensitive are the auto-weighting estimators to the choice of π and B? Could you provide guidelines or heuristics for selecting them in practice?  
5. Contradictory Behaviors in Figure 2: In Figure 2(a) vs. Figure 2(c), FedDA’s behavior appears contrasting; could you clarify whether this is due to dataset characteristics, hyperparameter settings, or a deeper theoretical reason?  
6. Non-i.i.d. and Non-smooth Losses: Your analysis assumes i.i.d. sampling and Lipschitz gradients. How would non-i.i.d. client data distributions or non-smooth objectives affect the performance of FedDA and FedGP?  
7. Privacy Considerations: Have you considered the privacy implications of transmitting multiple gradient batches for auto-weighting? Could gradient obfuscation or secure aggregation be integrated?

Recommendation  
Rating: 6 – marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
Overall, this submission makes a substantial contribution by providing the first fully principled theoretical framework for federated domain adaptation under data scarcity and domain shift. The novel FedDA and FedGP rules, along with their auto-weighting variants, are well motivated and empirically validated. While several practical aspects—such as scalability to multiple targets, overhead analysis, approximation accuracy, and privacy—warrant further discussion, the core ideas and results are significant and of interest to the federated learning and domain adaptation communities. I recommend acceptance with minor revisions addressing the points above.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the optimal design of an aggregator in federated domain generalization (generalizing to a target client/domain in cross-silo). The FedDA method works by aggregating interpolations (i.e. weighted averages) of the target domain gradient and the source domain gradients. The FedGP method further projects the source gradient onto the "positive direction" of the domain gradient before interpolation (i.e. zeroing out conflicting directions). The interpolation factor for each domain is chosen such that the $L^\pi$ distance between the source and target gradients is minimized w.r.t. some prior $\pi$ on the parameters. The authors show that this error can be decomposed as a noise term of the target domain and a distance term between the source and the target domains. The optimal interpolation factor would then balance the source and target gradients based on those terms, which can be estimated during training in a scheme called auto_weight. Extensive experiments show the benefits of this approach.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper's analysis and experiments are well-detailed.
- The analysis is interesting and covers many aspects of the design of an optimal aggregator in the federated domain generalization setting.
- The method is intuitive and is easy to implement (save for the auto-weighting scheme).
- The improvement seems to be significant in terms of generalizing to the target domain with respect to personalized federated learning algorithms.
reviews.weaknesses:
- It seems like it would be better to compare the algorithms presented in this paper to domain generalization algorithms, such as the ones shown in DomainBed's GitHub repo.
- The methods presented make sense mostly in the cross-silo setting, as mentioned in the paper, which limits its applicability to general federated learning problems with a relatively larger number of clients that can benefit a lot from methods for generalizing to new clients.
- It is mentioned multiple times that data scarcity is the setting of interest, in which FedDA and FedGP are supposed to perform more favorably. However, we do not see experiments showing the effect of data scarcity on the robustness of the performance of FedGP vs. FedAvg, for example.
- Personalized federated learning algorithms are relevant for comparison, but I think that direct comparison of such algorithms with FedGP might put them at a disadvantage since they are not specifically designed for domain generalization. ColoredMNIST, VLCS, and TerraIncognita datasets are more concerned with shifts in p(x) or p(x|y), whereas personalized FL is more concerned with shifts in p(y) and p(y|x), i.e. personalizing the prediction rather than adapting to spurious correlations or invariant attributes. You should either choose federated datasets for comparison, or you should compare your algorithm to domain generalization algorithms, e.g. IRM and others. Or why not use a hospital dataset that fits the setting you described in the paper? For example, you can consider FLamby [2].
- One work [1] from federated continual learning might be of interest (which even shares the same name FedGP). It is motivated from a similar intuition, which is to remove from the gradient its projection onto the negative direction of the reference gradient.
- In algorithm 1, the auto_weight scheme requires intermediate gradients for each domain, which might require a lot of memory.

[1] FedGP: Buffer-based Gradient Projection for Continual Federated Learning. Dai et al. 2023.
[2] FLamby: Datasets and Benchmarks for Cross-Silo Federated Learning in Realistic Healthcare Settings. Terrail et al. 2022.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the optimal design of an aggregator in federated domain generalization (generalizing to a target client/domain in cross-silo). The FedDA method works by aggregating interpolations (i.e. weighted averages) of the target domain gradient and the source domain gradients. The FedGP method further projects the source gradient onto the "positive direction" of the domain gradient before interpolation (i.e. zeroing out conflicting directions). The interpolation factor for each domain is chosen such that the $L^\pi$ distance between the source and target gradients is minimized w.r.t. some prior $\pi$ on the parameters. The authors show that this error can be decomposed as a noise term of the target domain and a distance term between the source and the target domains. The optimal interpolation factor would then balance the source and target gradients based on those terms, which can be estimated during training in a scheme called auto_weight. Extensive experiments show the benefits of this approach.

Strengths:
- The paper's analysis and experiments are well-detailed.
- The analysis is interesting and covers many aspects of the design of an optimal aggregator in the federated domain generalization setting.
- The method is intuitive and is easy to implement (save for the auto-weighting scheme).
- The improvement seems to be significant in terms of generalizing to the target domain with respect to personalized federated learning algorithms.

Weaknesses:
- It seems like it would be better to compare the algorithms presented in this paper to domain generalization algorithms, such as the ones shown in DomainBed's GitHub repo.
- The methods presented make sense mostly in the cross-silo setting, as mentioned in the paper, which limits its applicability to general federated learning problems with a relatively larger number of clients that can benefit a lot from methods for generalizing to new clients.
- It is mentioned multiple times that data scarcity is the setting of interest, in which FedDA and FedGP are supposed to perform more favorably. However, we do not see experiments showing the effect of data scarcity on the robustness of the performance of FedGP vs. FedAvg, for example.
- Personalized federated learning algorithms are relevant for comparison, but I think that direct comparison of such algorithms with FedGP might put them at a disadvantage since they are not specifically designed for domain generalization. ColoredMNIST, VLCS, and TerraIncognita datasets are more concerned with shifts in p(x) or p(x|y), whereas personalized FL is more concerned with shifts in p(y) and p(y|x), i.e. personalizing the prediction rather than adapting to spurious correlations or invariant attributes. You should either choose federated datasets for comparison, or you should compare your algorithm to domain generalization algorithms, e.g. IRM and others. Or why not use a hospital dataset that fits the setting you described in the paper? For example, you can consider FLamby [2].
- One work [1] from federated continual learning might be of interest (which even shares the same name FedGP). It is motivated from a similar intuition, which is to remove from the gradient its projection onto the negative direction of the reference gradient.
- In algorithm 1, the auto_weight scheme requires intermediate gradients for each domain, which might require a lot of memory.

[1] FedGP: Buffer-based Gradient Projection for Continual Federated Learning. Dai et al. 2023.
[2] FLamby: Datasets and Benchmarks for Cross-Silo Federated Learning in Realistic Healthcare Settings. Terrail et al. 2022.

Questions:
Can you train your algorithms and compare them on federated datasets that follows the setting of interest in the paper (cross-silo with data scarcity)?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper tackles the problem of federated domain generalization in a cross-silo setting, where a single target client with scarce labeled data must benefit from multiple source clients under domain shift. Building on a rigorous theoretical framework, the authors introduce the Δ-error, which decomposes the gap between aggregated and true target gradients into a domain‐distance term (an Lπ metric) and a target‐variance term. They analyze generic aggregation rules and then propose two concrete methods: FedDA, which takes a convex combination of source and target gradients, and FedGP, which further projects out conflicting source‐gradient components along the target‐gradient “positive direction.” Closed‐form expressions for the optimal interpolation weights yield an “auto-weighting” scheme that balances the bias–variance trade-off. The paper provides convergence guarantees and unbiased estimators for the key quantities, and validates both methods through extensive synthetic and real‐world experiments (ColoredMNIST, VLCS, TerraIncognita, DomainNet, PACS, Office-Home), showing consistent gains over FedAvg, personalized FL, UFDA, and unsupervised domain generalization baselines.

Strengths:  
1. Theoretical Rigor and Novel Metrics:  
   ­– Introducing the Δ-error and linking aggregation performance to a principled Lπ source–target distance and target‐variance term is a significant advance.  
   ­– The derivations of convergence and generalization bounds underpin both FedDA and FedGP, and support the closed‐form auto-weighting rules.  
   ­– The theoretical predictions closely match observed performance in controlled synthetic shifts, lending credibility to the framework.  

2. Methodological Clarity and Practicality:  
   ­– FedDA and FedGP are conceptually intuitive and require only server‐side modifications, making them straightforward to implement in cross-silo FL systems.  
   ­– FedGP’s projection rule neatly filters out gradient directions that conflict with the target, addressing spurious correlations—a valuable contribution over heuristic personalization schemes.  

3. Comprehensive Empirical Validation:  
   ­– The authors evaluate on a wide range of benchmarks and compare against strong baselines, including personalized federated learning algorithms, UFDA, and domain generalization methods.  
   ­– Experiments demonstrate significant improvements under realistic data scarcity scenarios, confirming that auto‐weighted aggregation consistently outperforms unweighted or heuristic combinations.  

Weaknesses:  
1. Limited Comparison to Domain Generalization Methods:  
   ­– While personalized FL baselines are included, the paper would benefit from direct comparisons to popular domain generalization and invariant learning approaches (e.g. IRM, GroupDRO) available in DomainBed. This would clarify how much the gains stem from federated design versus established DG techniques.  

2. Applicability Beyond Cross-Silo:  
   ­– The methods rely on computing intermediate gradients for each domain in the auto-weighting step, which may incur substantial memory, computation, and communication overhead if the number of source clients grows large. Quantifying this cost and exploring lightweight approximations or batching strategies is needed for broader FL scalability.  

3. Sensitivity to Hyperparameters and Approximations:  
   ­– The auto-weight estimators depend on the choice of batch size B and the prior π on parameter space. The paper currently lacks an empirical study of this sensitivity in realistic FL deployments. Similarly, the analysis invokes mean-field and high-dimensional approximations (τ≈const, δ≈1), but does not quantify the approximation error or its impact on weight selection in practice.  

4. Dataset Scope and Realism:  
   ­– Experiments focus on well-known vision benchmarks with synthetic shifts. Incorporating realistic cross-silo federated datasets—such as FLamby for healthcare—or larger non-image modalities would strengthen the paper’s claims about robustness to data scarcity and domain shift in real deployments.  

5. Presentation Density:  
   ­– The manuscript is dense with heavy notation; key derivations (especially for the auto-weighting formulas) are deferred to the appendix. A concise schematic or intuitive summary in the main text would improve readability and reproducibility.  

Questions for the Authors:  
1. Can you provide guidelines or empirical results on how sensitive FedDA/FedGP performance is to the choice of π and batch size B?  
2. How does the computational and communication overhead of auto-weighting scale when the number of source clients is large? Are there effective approximations to reduce the required gradients or memory footprint?  
3. Have you considered extending the framework to multi-target settings, where several scarce clients adapt simultaneously?  
4. Could you clarify the privacy implications of sending multiple local update vectors for weight estimation, and whether secure aggregation techniques could mitigate any concerns?  
5. Would comparisons to unsupervised domain generalization baselines (e.g. IRM, GroupDRO) using DomainBed yield additional insight into the unique benefits of your federated approach?  

Conclusion and Rating:  
Overall, this paper presents a principled and well-validated framework for federated domain generalization under target data scarcity. The theoretical contributions—particularly the Δ-error decomposition and auto-weighting scheme—are novel and supported by extensive experiments. However, practical considerations around broader baseline comparisons, dataset realism, hyperparameter sensitivity, and scalability warrant further investigation. I therefore recommend a marginal acceptance (rating: 6), contingent on addressing the above points to bolster the paper’s applicability and clarity.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers two important issues in federated learning: small data at client sites and domain shift across clients. Simple, intuitive strategies such as gradient projection and auto-weighting for mitigating these issues are proposed. Several interesting theorems are proved regarding federated aggregation, gradient projectin. Results on three datasets are provided.

Strengths:
Nice treatment of federated learning in the presence of domain shift and small data. A good mixture of theoretical and experimental work.

Weaknesses:
I loved the paper till I came to the experiments section. In this day and age, should we still be doing experiments with ColoredMNIST, VLCS, CIFAR-10 and TerraIncognita? ColoredMNIST, VLCS and TerraIngocnita are from 2019, 2013 and 2018 respectively! This raises the questions whether the proposed solutions will scale to larger and difficult datasets.

Questions:
Try on DomainNet, Office Home and PACS datasets. Although these datasets are from 2019 and 2017, atleast they are more challenging datasets.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper tackles federated domain adaptation (FDA) in cross-silo settings where a small target client has limited labeled data and suffers domain shift relative to multiple source clients. The authors introduce a theoretical framework that defines two key metrics—source–target gradient distance and target gradient variance—and aggregate them into a squared “Delta error” that governs any aggregation rule’s performance. Building on this analysis, they propose two server‐side aggregation strategies: FedDA, which forms a convex combination of source and target gradients with closed‐form auto‐weighting, and FedGP, which filters and projects source gradients along the target gradient direction before combination. Theoretical contributions include convergence and generalization bounds, a precise bias–variance decomposition of the Delta error, and unbiased estimators for the required quantities. Empirically, the methods are evaluated on synthetic shifts and four image‐classification benchmarks (ColoredMNIST, VLCS, CIFAR-10, TerraIncognita).

Strengths:
• Novel theoretical lens.  By explicitly relating FDA performance to a Delta error decomposed into domain‐distance and variance terms, the paper offers clear predictive insights and advances over heuristic aggregation rules in personalized FL or unsupervised FDA.  
• Elegant aggregation rules.  FedDA and FedGP follow directly from the theory, and the closed‐form auto‐weighting schemes optimize the bias–variance trade‐off without client‐side code changes.  
• Rigorous analysis.  Theorems on convergence and Delta‐error decomposition are cleanly stated, and the paper derives unbiased estimators for the newly introduced metrics.  
• Promising empirical gains.  Across synthetic experiments and four popular benchmarks, FedGP (and its auto‐weighted variants) consistently outperforms FedAvg, personalization baselines, UFDA, and domain generalization methods, particularly under severe shifts.

Weaknesses:
• Limited experimental scale.  The reliance on ColoredMNIST, VLCS, CIFAR-10, and TerraIncognita—datasets dating from 2013–2019—raises concerns about scalability to larger, more challenging domains.  In particular, widely‐adopted FDA benchmarks like DomainNet, PACS, and Office-Home are missing.  It remains unclear whether the proposed schemes maintain their advantages in higher-resolution, multi-class settings or with non‐image modalities.  
• Heavy notation and dense presentation.  Key derivations (e.g. the auto‐weight estimator and the mean‐field approximations underlying FedGP) are deferred to the appendix, making the core contributions harder to digest.  A high‐level schematic or simplified summary in the main text would aid readability.  
• Strong assumptions and approximations.  The theoretical framework relies on mean‐field and high‐dimensional assumptions (τ≈const, δ≈1) and i.i.d. sampling with Lipschitz gradients.  The gap between these approximations and real‐world FDA scenarios is not quantified, leaving open questions about robustness when clients’ data distributions are non‐i.i.d. or losses are non­-smooth.  
• Overhead and scalability.  The auto‐weighting procedure requires multiple gradient batches from the target client and storage of per-source metric estimates.  The computational and communication costs—as well as potential privacy implications of transmitting multiple local updates—are not discussed in depth, limiting practical adopters’ ability to judge feasibility in large‐scale cross‐silo deployments.  
• Hyperparameter sensitivity.  The choice of gradient‐batch size B and distribution π directly affects weight estimation, yet guidelines or sensitivity analyses are absent.  It is unclear how practitioners should select π or how robust the method is to this choice.

Detailed Suggestions:
1. Expand experiments to larger FDA benchmarks such as DomainNet, PACS, and Office-Home to demonstrate scalability and assess performance under higher-dimensional shifts.  
2. Provide a concise summary of the auto‐weighting derivation in the main body—perhaps via a flowchart or boxed algorithm—to clarify implementation steps and improve accessibility.  
3. Empirically quantify the error introduced by mean‐field and high‐dimensional approximations in FedGP’s Delta‐error bounds, for example by comparing predicted versus actual optimal weights on held‐out shifts.  
4. Analyze the computational and communication overhead of auto‐weighting in a setting with tens or hundreds of source clients, and discuss potential lightweight approximations or batching strategies to reduce server‐side load.  
5. Include a sensitivity study on the distribution π and batch count B, offering practical guidelines for their selection in real federated deployments.  
6. Consider privacy ramifications of sending multiple gradient vectors from the target client; if necessary, suggest aggregation or noise‐addition schemes that preserve DP guarantees.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold. While the paper presents a rigorous and elegant theoretical framework with promising preliminary results, its limited experimental scope, dense presentation, and insufficient discussion of scalability and practical overhead suggest that further refinement is needed before acceptance.

title: Consistency-guided Prompt Learning for Vision-Language Models

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new adaptation method for CLIP like large scale vision-language models for generalization benchmarks. Specifically, the authors propose 3 techniques to improve generalization of CLIP. Firstly, they observe that the main cause of poor generalization is the lack of consistency constraints between the learned embeddings and the original pretrained embeddings. To overcome this issue, consistency losses are used at the text side as well as the image side separately. Secondly, the inputs to the original models are perturbed with the help of augmentations and LLM captions for image and text side respectively. Lastly, the proposed method combines the adapter and prompt learning modules with-in the same architecture for improved performance. 

Extensive benchmark comparisons are conduced on 3 different generalization tasks where the proposed approach shows improvements against prior methods. Furthermore, ablation studies are provided for analyzing contributions of each component separately and motivating the design choices.
reviews.strengths:
1) This paper addresses an important aspect of generalization of pre-trained CLIP like models for downstream task adaptation. Most of the prior methods struggles to achieve good performance on unseen classes and datasets, while this method explicitly add training constraints to mitigate the issue.
2) The proposed framework is motivated fairly, and the strength of its individual components have been demonstrated clearly in the ablation studies.
3) The method shows impressive performance against the previous prompt learning methods.
4) Paper is easy to read.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The authors mentioned that their baseline is MaPLe, which uses coupling functions between vision and text branches, but in Figure 3, no coupling functions are visible. It will be good to clarify the exact architecture used in the proposed framework. Also I think there is graphic error in image encoder as the visual prompts (orange color) are not shown in intermediate layers of CLIP visual encoder. 

2. It will be good to see the proposed method generalization for a newer V-L model. CLIP is relatively outdated and the authors are encouraged to show result on at least another recent CLIP variant. For example on EVA-CLIP[1] model. 

3. There is a recent prompt learning method PromptSRC [2], which also seems to introduce consistency constraints to prompt learning to improve generalization. How is the proposed method different from this work? Also, all fair comparisons should be added in the main paper. 

4. The diagrams in the paper are of very poor quality. Specially the text in the Figure 2. graph is very small and the color scheme used is confusing. Also in the Figure 1, their is no indication of using adapters in Fig. 1b. 

5. I think there is some writing logical errors in the paper. For example, in the Adapters heading in section 3, adapter based method are being mentioned but prompts have been written instead of the adapter blocks. 

[1] Exploring the Limits of Masked Visual Representation Learning at Scale (CVPR-23)
[2] Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting (ICCV-23)
reviews.questions: Please refer to the weaknesses section for additional questions and queries!
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
