Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents CoPrompt, a parameter-efficient adaptation strategy for CLIP-like vision–language models aimed at improving generalization on few-shot and zero-shot benchmarks. Building on the observation that fine-tuning often leads to drift from the original pretrained representations, the authors introduce three key components: (1) cosine-based consistency losses on both image and text embeddings to align the tunable branch with the frozen encoder; (2) multimodal input perturbations—simple image augmentations and LLM-generated descriptive text—to regularize each branch; and (3) the joint integration of prompt learning and lightweight adapters within the same architecture. Extensive experiments across base-to-novel generalization, cross-dataset zero-shot transfer, and domain generalization on up to 11 benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over prior methods (CoOp, CoCoOp, MaPLe, PromptSRC), achieving new state-of-the-art harmonic-mean accuracies. Detailed ablation studies isolate the contributions of each component and motivate design choices.

Strengths  
1. Importance and Originality: The work addresses a critical challenge in few-shot adaptation of large-scale vision–language models—preserving zero-shot capabilities while improving novel-class accuracy. Although consistency regularization, adapters, and prompts have appeared individually, their joint design with LLM-driven text perturbations is novel and well motivated.  
2. Empirical Performance: CoPrompt yields up to ~2% improvement in novel-class or harmonic-mean accuracy across diverse benchmarks, often outperforming recent methods such as PromptSRC. It sets new state-of-the-art results on base-to-novel generalization, cross-dataset transfer, and domain generalization tasks.  
3. Thorough Analysis: The paper provides extensive ablations and sensitivity studies—varying loss weights (λ), perturbation types, adapter depths, and shot counts—to validate each module’s impact. These analyses clarify why simple augmentations can outperform stronger schemes (e.g., RandAug).  
4. Clarity and Reproducibility: The methodology is clearly described, the writing is generally accessible, and the authors plan to release code. Figures, especially the high-level overview (Fig. 1), convey the training workflow concisely.

Weaknesses  
1. Architectural Details and Diagrams: While the main text cites MaPLe-style coupling functions between vision and text branches, Fig. 3 omits any explicit coupling. It would help to clarify the precise architecture—particularly where visual prompts (orange blocks) are inserted—and correct graphical inconsistencies in Fig. 1b and Fig. 2.  
2. Evaluation Scope: The paper focuses solely on vanilla CLIP. Given the rapid evolution of vision–language backbones, adding results on a recent variant (e.g., EVA-CLIP) would strengthen claims of broad applicability. Similarly, assessing CoPrompt on tasks beyond classification—such as retrieval or segmentation—could demonstrate generalization to other modalities.  
3. Comparison to Related Work: PromptSRC (ICCV ’23) also employs consistency constraints for prompt learning. A more detailed comparison—ideally including its results in the main tables—would clarify CoPrompt’s unique advantages.  
4. Computational Overhead: On-the-fly LLM calls for text perturbations and dual forward passes of the frozen model incur nontrivial runtime and memory costs. While ablations touch on this, a quantitative discussion of training/inference overhead and scalability to larger shot counts or backbones is missing.  
5. Theoretical Justification and Notation: The choice of cosine distance over alternatives (e.g., KL divergence) is empirically driven but would benefit from deeper justification. In addition, some sections have minor typos and notation inconsistencies (e.g., mixed symbols for loss terms in Equations 3–5), and logical slips—such as referring to prompts instead of adapters under the “Adapters” heading—should be addressed.

Questions for the Authors  
1. Architectural Clarification: Can you specify where coupling functions between branches are applied and correct the depiction of visual prompts in the encoder diagram?  
2. Generalization to New Backbones: Have you tried CoPrompt on newer CLIP variants (e.g., EVA-CLIP) or larger ViT backbones?  
3. Consistency Variants: Could the consistency loss be extended to hidden-layer features or alternative metrics (e.g., KL divergence), and what effect might this have on performance?  
4. Hyperparameter Sensitivity: How robust is CoPrompt to the choice of λ across different datasets and shot regimes? Can a single λ generalize well in practice?  
5. Computational Costs: What are the end-to-end training and inference overheads introduced by LLM-based text augmentation and dual-model consistency? How do these scale with shot count and model size?  
6. Broader Applicability: Have you considered applying CoPrompt to non-classification tasks (e.g., retrieval, segmentation) or other modalities (e.g., audio–text)?

Conclusion and Rating  
Overall, CoPrompt is a well-motivated and technically sound contribution that achieves state-of-the-art generalization for few-shot and zero-shot adaptation of vision–language models. The joint use of consistency losses, multimodal perturbations, and combined adapters/prompts is novel and thoroughly validated. While concerns remain regarding architectural clarity, broader evaluation, and computational overhead, these can be addressed in a revision. I recommend acceptance (rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold).

Also I think there is graphic error in image encoder as the visual prompts (orange color) are not shown in intermediate layers of CLIP visual encoder. 

2. It will be good to see the proposed method generalization for a newer V-L model. CLIP is relatively outdated and the authors are encouraged to show result on at least another recent CLIP variant. For example on EVA-CLIP[1] model. 

3. There is a recent prompt learning method PromptSRC [2], which also seems to introduce consistency constraints to prompt learning to improve generalization. How is the proposed method different from this work? Also, all fair comparisons should be added in the main paper. 

4. The diagrams in the paper are of very poor quality. Specially the text in the Figure 2. graph is very small and the color scheme used is confusing. Also in the Figure 1, their is no indication of using adapters in Fig. 1b. 

5. I think there is some writing logical errors in the paper. For example, in the Adapters heading in section 3, adapter based method are being mentioned but prompts have been written instead of the adapter blocks. 

[1] Exploring the Limits of Masked Visual Representation Learning at Scale (CVPR-23)
[2] Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting (ICCV-23)

reviews.summary: The paper presents an innovative prompt learning technique that integrates a consistency mechanism between trainable and pre-trained models to mitigate the risk of overfitting. This approach employs a consistency term applied to two altered inputs within the text and visual spheres. In text modality, the method leverages existing language models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 to introduce variations, whereas for images, it uses standard image augmentation techniques prevalent in self-supervised learning. The authors have skillfully merged two distinct approaches to adaptation - prompting and adaptation - demonstrating that this synergy, coupled with a consistency loss, enhances the method's ability to generalize. The improved generalization capability of this approach is evident in various prompt learning tasks, including adapting from base to new tasks, cross-dataset evaluation, and domain generalization, with consistent enhancements observed across these applications.
reviews.strengths: The paper is clear and of high quality, with significant numerical results. The authors offer a thorough analysis of their proposed method's various components, which overall appear sensible and well-founded.
reviews.weaknesses:
While the paper is clear, and the numerical results are noteworthy, its novelty isn't entirely clear. The paper's self-consistency terms seem similar to those in self-supervised learning (SSL) methods. The authors' claim of differentiating their approach from SSL, where two perturbed inputs within a single encoder are used, doesn't fully convince. In SSL, typically there are two encoders: an online encoder and a momentum encoder. The paper’s pre-trained and trainable encoders appear analogous to SSL’s momentum and online encoders, respectively. The authors should clarify this similarity.

Additionally, the paper omits recent relevant studies like Bayesian Prompt Learning [1] and Prompt Distribution Learning [2], which address overfitting in vision and language models. Discussing these in the related work and comparing them in sections like domain generalization are necessary, especially given that in some cases, such as Bayesian Prompt Learning, they outperform the methods in this paper. For example, in the domain generalization task, the Bayesian Prompt Learning method (%60.44) works better than the paper performance (%60.42). 

[1]. Bayesian Prompt Learning for Image-Language Model Generalization, ICCV 2023

[2]. Prompt Distribution Learning, CVPR 2022

reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper proposes a consistency-guided prompt learning framework that aims to mitigate overfitting when fine-tuning large pre-trained vision–language models in few-shot settings. A frozen “pre-trained” encoder and a tunable “prompt+adapter” encoder process two perturbed views—augmented images and LLM-generated text descriptions—and a cosine-based consistency loss encourages their embeddings to remain close. The method elegantly unites prompting and lightweight adapters with self-supervised–style consistency regularization, and the authors demonstrate consistent gains in base-to-novel generalization, cross-dataset zero-shot transfer, and domain generalization across 11 benchmarks.

Strengths:  
1. Clear Motivation and Quality of Presentation  
   - The paper is well written, with clear diagrams and a thorough methodological description that make the consistency mechanism and multimodal perturbations easy to follow.  
   - The inclusion of prompt tuning alongside adapter modules provides a flexible yet parameter-efficient adaptation strategy.  

2. Empirical Rigor and Significance  
   - Extensive experiments—including base-to-novel harmonic-mean accuracy, cross-dataset transfer, and domain generalization—show consistent improvements over strong baselines like CoOp, CoCoOp, MaPLe, and PromptSRC.  
   - Detailed ablations isolate the contributions of the consistency loss, image augmentations, LLM-based text perturbations, and adapter depth, supporting the claim that each component is beneficial.  
   - The method often sets new state-of-the-art performance in few-shot novel-class accuracy without sacrificing base-class performance, demonstrating practical value for real-world few-shot adaptation.  

3. Reproducibility and Broader Impact  
   - The authors release code and provide sufficient implementation details, enhancing reproducibility.  
   - The framework is general and could readily extend to other vision–language backbones or modalities, which is likely to influence future prompt-based fine-tuning strategies.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. Novelty and Relation to Self-Supervised Learning  
   - The consistency term bears strong resemblance to self-supervised learning (SSL) approaches that use an online encoder and a momentum encoder on two perturbed inputs. In this framing, the frozen and tunable encoders parallel the momentum and online networks, respectively. The authors’ effort to distinguish their work from SSL would benefit from a more explicit discussion of these parallels and a clearer articulation of how their consistency objective or training dynamics differ from, for example, BYOL or SimSiam.  

2. Missing Citations and Empirical Comparisons  
   - Important recent works on prompt overfitting—such as Bayesian Prompt Learning (ICCV 2023) and Prompt Distribution Learning (CVPR 2022)—are omitted. Bayesian Prompt Learning even slightly outperforms the current method on domain generalization (60.44% vs. 60.42%). A comparative discussion in the related work, as well as an empirical head-to-head evaluation, would contextualize this paper’s contributions more fully.  

3. Computational and Inference Overhead  
   - While LLM-generated text perturbations yield gains, invoking an external language model on-the-fly can be computationally expensive. The paper would be strengthened by reporting training time, memory usage, and inference latency compared to standard prompt tuning or adapter methods. Is the overhead from dual model forward passes and LLM calls prohibitive in practice? Can these costs be mitigated?  

4. Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Theoretical Justification  
   - The choice of the consistency weight λ and the use of cosine distance over other metrics (e.g., KL divergence) are empirically driven but lack theoretical grounding. How sensitive are results to λ across datasets? Could a single λ generalize, or does it require dataset-specific tuning? A brief sensitivity study or justification would guide practitioners.  

5. Clarity and Minor Presentation Issues  
   - Some notation in loss definitions and subscripts is dense or inconsistent; for instance, Equations 3–5 mix symbols for image and text losses. A concise notation table or schematic of the full training loop would improve readability.  

Questions for Authors:  
1. Can you clarify how your frozen vs. tunable encoder setup differs fundamentally from the momentum vs. online design in SSL frameworks?  
2. Have you evaluated the training/inference overhead (runtime, memory) introduced by LLM-based perturbations and dual forward passes?  
3. How does performance scale with larger shot counts (e.g., 32 or 64)? Do gains persist or diminish?  
4. Beyond GPT-2/3, have you tried other LLMs for text augmentation, and does the choice significantly affect results?  
5. Could this consistency mechanism extend to hidden-layer features or other modalities (e.g., audio-text)?  

Rating: 6 – marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
Overall, this work offers a compelling integration of consistency regularization, multimodal perturbations, and prompt+adapter fine-tuning that yields consistent state-of-the-art gains. Clarifications on novelty relative to SSL, broader comparisons to recent prompt-overfitting methods, and more detail on computational trade-offs would further solidify its contribution.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a consistency-guided prompt learning (CoPrompt) method to transfer CLIP to downstream tasks in a few-shot setting. Experimental results show the capacity of consistency-guided prompt learning to imporve the generalization comparing with the SOTA methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. CoPrompt achieves the SOTA results on base-to-novel generalization and cross-domain setting. 

2. Ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of consistency constrain to prevent overfitting on the downstream tasks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1.The prompt learning and adapter learning method mentioned in this paper are introduced by MaPLe and CLIP-Adapter. The primary contribution of this paper only lies in the introduction of consistency constraint learning.  Thus, regarding the method as prompt learning is a bit ambiguous in my opinion.

2. The comparison between CoPrompt and Zero-shot CLIP is not fair enought. The diverse text prompts generated by LLM can imporve the zero-shot classification ability of CLIP on downstream tasks. It is important to consider this aspect when evaluating the performance of  Zero-shot CLIP.

3. On small scale dataset like Eurosat, the higher value of λ leads to worse performance, accroding to Table 7. However, the analysis regarding this observation is missing from the paper. Can CoPrompt reaches better result on Eurosat if λ=0?
reviews.questions:
1. What if combined consistency constrain learning with other existing methods, like CoCoOP. Can CoPrompt improve the generalization of CoCoOP?

2. What is the training overhead in terms of time? What is training and test-time inference speed compared with prior methods?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a consistency-guided prompt learning (CoPrompt) method to transfer CLIP to downstream tasks in a few-shot setting. Experimental results show the capacity of consistency-guided prompt learning to imporve the generalization comparing with the SOTA methods.

Strengths:
1. CoPrompt achieves the SOTA results on base-to-novel generalization and cross-domain setting. 

2. Ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of consistency constrain to prevent overfitting on the downstream tasks.

Weaknesses:
1.The prompt learning and adapter learning method mentioned in this paper are introduced by MaPLe and CLIP-Adapter. The primary contribution of this paper only lies in the introduction of consistency constraint learning.  Thus, regarding the method as prompt learning is a bit ambiguous in my opinion.

2. The comparison between CoPrompt and Zero-shot CLIP is not fair enought. The diverse text prompts generated by LLM can imporve the zero-shot classification ability of CLIP on downstream tasks. It is important to consider this aspect when evaluating the performance of  Zero-shot CLIP.

3. On small scale dataset like Eurosat, the higher value of λ leads to worse performance, accroding to Table 7. However, the analysis regarding this observation is missing from the paper. Can CoPrompt reaches better result on Eurosat if λ=0?

Questions:
1. What if combined consistency constrain learning with other existing methods, like CoCoOP. Can CoPrompt improve the generalization of CoCoOP?

2. What is the training overhead in terms of time? What is training and test-time inference speed compared with prior methods?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper proposes Consistency-guided Prompt learning (CoPrompt), a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework that adapts frozen vision–language models (e.g., CLIP) to few-shot downstream tasks. CoPrompt imposes a cosine-based consistency constraint between embeddings from the frozen pre-trained encoder and those from a tunable prompt-and-adapter branch, applied on both image and text modalities. Input perturbations—simple image augmentations and LLM-generated descriptive prompts—are used to regularize each branch. Extensive experiments on base-to-novel generalization, cross-dataset zero-shot transfer, and domain generalization across 11 benchmarks demonstrate that CoPrompt consistently outperforms existing prompt- and adapter-based methods (CoOp, CoCoOp, MaPLe, CLIP-Adapter), achieving new state-of-the-art harmonic-mean accuracies. Ablation studies further isolate the contributions of the consistency loss, perturbations, and multi-layer adapters.

Strengths:  
1. Empirical Gains: CoPrompt achieves leading results on base-to-novel and cross-domain few-shot settings, and preserves zero-shot performance. The reported improvements (up to ~2% harmonic-mean gain) are consistent across 11 benchmarks, demonstrating robust generalization.  
2. Thorough Evaluation: The paper delivers comprehensive ablation studies and sensitivity analyses, validating each component (consistency constraint, image/text perturbations, adapter depth). These experiments convincingly show that the consistency regularizer effectively mitigates overfitting in low-shot regimes.  
3. Clear Presentation and Reproducibility: The methodology is well motivated, with clear algorithmic descriptions and detailed appendices. Public release of code and model configurations further supports reproducibility.  
4. Practical Significance: By combining prompts and lightweight adapters under a consistency framework, CoPrompt offers a broadly applicable strategy for efficient adaptation of vision–language backbones without full-model tuning.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. Novelty Scope: While the integration of consistency regularization, LLM-driven text augmentation, and adapters/prompts is well executed, the constituent components draw heavily on prior works (MaPLe, CLIP-Adapter, CoCoOp). A deeper discussion clarifying how CoPrompt’s joint design substantially diverges from or extends these methods would strengthen the paper’s novelty claim.  
2. Fairness of Baselines: The comparison to zero-shot CLIP omits recent advances that leverage diverse text prompts generated by large language models. Including such LLM-augmented zero-shot baselines would ensure a more equitable evaluation of few-shot gains.  
3. Hyperparameter Sensitivity: Table 7 shows that higher λ degrades performance on small datasets (e.g., EuroSAT), yet no analysis is provided. A brief study on λ’s optimal range and whether setting λ=0 might yield better small-scale results would clarify best practices.  
4. Computational Overhead: On-the-fly LLM text generation and dual forward passes introduce nontrivial runtime and memory costs. Quantifying training and inference time overhead relative to CoOp/CLIP-Adapter and discussing potential strategies to reduce this burden (e.g., cached prompts) would address practical deployment concerns.  
5. Generalization to Other Tasks: The current scope is classification. Exploring whether CoPrompt extends to retrieval, detection, or segmentation tasks—or clarifying any limitations in such domains—would broaden the method’s impact.  
6. Theoretical Justification: The choice of cosine distance for consistency is motivated empirically; a succinct theoretical rationale or comparison with alternative metrics (e.g., KL divergence) could solidify the method’s underpinnings.  

Minor Comments:  
• Notation in Equations 3–5 is occasionally dense; consistent subscripts and a notation table would improve clarity.  
• Clarify whether CoPrompt can be combined with advanced prompt methods like CoCoOp to further boost generalization.  

Decision and Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold  
CoPrompt represents a solid empirical contribution to few-shot adaptation of vision–language models, offering consistent state-of-the-art gains through a well-designed consistency framework. While certain aspects—baseline fairness, hyperparameter analysis, and computational costs—warrant further elaboration, the paper’s strengths in methodological clarity, extensive evaluation, and practical relevance justify acceptance.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a consistency-enforced fine-tuning method for large foundation model CLIP that enables learning a new task from a few samples while maintaining the zero-shot generalizability. The proposed method incorporates the knowledge of a pretrained LLM with consistency constraints on the text branch and data augmentations on the image branch to improve the generalization further along with learnable adaptors on both image and text branches.
reviews.strengths:
- The  paper is well-written and easy to follow.
- The authors have shown decent results on base-to-novel generalization.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The idea of adaptors and prompt-tuning already exist in the literature. Merely combining the two ideas seems an incremental work and not novel.
- The idea of retaining the generalizability of the CLIP using consistency loss has already been explored in the paper "Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting" (ICCV 2023) [1]. Hence,  the consistency loss doesn't contribute towards the novelty.
- The authors have not compared their approach to the above paper and there is also no reference to the paper.
- The improvements in the Domain generalization is marginal given that authors have fine-tuned the model. Same is true for cross-dataset evaluation.


[1] Muhammad Uzair Khattak, Syed Talal Wasim, Muzammal Naseer, Salman Khan, Ming-Hsuan Yang, Fahad Shahbaz Khan. Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting. ICCV 2023 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.06948)
reviews.questions: - Are the vision side prompts conditioned on text side? Do authors follow MaPLe settings or Independent VL prompting?

Summary:
The paper proposes a consistency-enforced fine-tuning method for large foundation model CLIP that enables learning a new task from a few samples while maintaining the zero-shot generalizability. The proposed method incorporates the knowledge of a pretrained LLM with consistency constraints on the text branch and data augmentations on the image branch to improve the generalization further along with learnable adaptors on both image and text branches.

Strengths:
- The  paper is well-written and easy to follow.
- The authors have shown decent results on base-to-novel generalization.

Weaknesses:
- The idea of adaptors and prompt-tuning already exist in the literature. Merely combining the two ideas seems an incremental work and not novel.
- The idea of retaining the generalizability of the CLIP using consistency loss has already been explored in the paper "Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting" (ICCV 2023) [1]. Hence,  the consistency loss doesn't contribute towards the novelty.
- The authors have not compared their approach to the above paper and there is also no reference to the paper.
- The improvements in the Domain generalization is marginal given that authors have fine-tuned the model. Same is true for cross-dataset evaluation.


[1] Muhammad Uzair Khattak, Syed Talal Wasim, Muzammal Naseer, Salman Khan, Ming-Hsuan Yang, Fahad Shahbaz Khan. Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting. ICCV 2023 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.06948)

Questions:
- Are the vision side prompts conditioned on text side? Do authors follow MaPLe settings or Independent VL prompting?

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper presents a fine-tuning framework—dubbed CoPrompt—that enforces consistency between embeddings of a frozen CLIP model and a tunable branch via a cosine-based loss on both image and text representations. The approach augments images with standard data transforms and leverages LLM-generated descriptive text to regularize the text branch, while also incorporating learnable adapters and prompts on both modalities. Evaluations cover few-shot base-to-novel generalization, cross-dataset zero-shot transfer, and domain generalization across 11 benchmarks, reporting modest improvements over prior prompt- and adapter-based methods.

Strengths:
- Clarity and Presentation: Consistent with prior reviews, the manuscript is well written, well structured, and easy to follow; figures and tables are informative, and the appendices provide reproducibility details.
- Empirical Rigor: Extensive experiments and ablation studies isolate the contributions of consistency regularization, input perturbations, and the joint use of adapters and prompts. The reported gains—up to ~2% in novel-class or harmonic-mean accuracy—are consistent across multiple datasets.
- Comprehensive Comparisons: The work benchmarks against a wide range of prompt- and adapter-based baselines (e.g., CoOp, CoCoOp, MaPLe, PromptSRC), demonstrating consistent—even if incremental—improvements.
- Potential Impact: By preserving zero-shot generalizability while improving few-shot adaptation, the method addresses a pertinent challenge in vision-language model fine-tuning and may inspire future prompt-and-adapter designs.

Weaknesses:
- Novelty and Prior Art: The core components—prompt tuning, adapters, and consistency losses—have each been explored in recent literature. In particular, the concept of self-regulating prompts to prevent forgetting (Khattak et al., ICCV 2023) employs a similar consistency constraint, yet this key prior work is neither cited nor compared. Without proper discussion and empirical contrast to this paper, the consistency loss offers limited novelty.
- Incremental Gains vs. Complexity: The absolute improvements are often marginal (<2%), and the addition of LLM-driven text augmentation introduces nontrivial computational overhead. Prior alternative reviews rightly point out concerns over dual forward passes of frozen encoders and on-the-fly LLM calls, which may hinder scalability in practice.
- Theoretical Justification and Hyperparameter Sensitivity: While thorough ablations validate design choices, there is little theoretical rationale for selecting cosine distance over other metrics or for setting loss weights (λ) uniformly across tasks. Sensitivity analyses in alternative reviews suggest hyperparameters can be dataset-specific, raising concerns about general applicability.
- Limited Scope of Evaluation: The paper focuses solely on classification tasks with up to 16 shots. There is no exploration of higher-shot regimes, other backbone variants (e.g., larger ViTs or alternative CLIP models), or tasks beyond classification (e.g., retrieval, dense prediction), which would strengthen claims of broad utility.
- Notational and Clarity Issues: Minor typos and inconsistent use of symbols for loss terms occasionally hamper readability. A schematic of the full training loop and clearer definitions of variables would aid reproducibility, as highlighted in prior feedback.

Questions for the Authors:
1. How does CoPrompt performance and computational overhead scale when using larger LLMs for text perturbations or when increasing the number of shots beyond 16?
2. Can you provide a direct comparison—both empirical and conceptual—to “Self-regulating Prompts” (ICCV 2023) and clarify how your consistency loss differs or improves upon it?
3. Have you experimented with alternative distance metrics (e.g., KL divergence) or applying consistency constraints at intermediate feature layers?
4. What strategies can mitigate the extra compute and latency introduced by dual-model consistency checks and LLM-generated text during training?
5. Could a single set of hyperparameters (especially λ) be robustly applied across all benchmarks, or is per-dataset tuning required?

Decision:
Rating: 5 (marginally below acceptance threshold)

While CoPrompt is well executed and delivers consistent empirical gains, its contributions are incremental given the existing literature on adapters, prompt tuning, and consistency-based regularization. The lack of comparison to closely related work, marginal improvements relative to added complexity, and limited theoretical grounding suggest that the paper falls just below the bar for acceptance in its current form.

title: Explaining Time Series via Contrastive and Locally Sparse Perturbations

reviews.summary:
This paper presents ContraLSP, a locally sparse model that introduces counterfactual samples to build uninformative perturbations but keeps distribution using contrastive learning.

Note that this paper does not quite match the expertise of my research and I have made the comments to AC.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of using contrastive loss seems to be a new idea
2. In the experiment section, the authors provide a comprehensive comparison with multiple baseline models
3. The paper is well written.
reviews.weaknesses:
I mainly have some questions to the author:
1. I curious about if the method is scalable to high dimension data. For example, video sequences?
2. What is the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ values you use for each dataset? and how do you determine their values?
3. In section 4.1, I wonder why you choose Manhattan distance rather than more conventional Euclidean distance? Optionally, other metrics like cosine distance might work better for contrastive learning?
reviews.questions: see above

2. What is the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ values you use for each dataset? and how do you determine their values?
3. In section 4.1, I wonder why you choose Manhattan distance rather than more conventional Euclidean distance? Optionally, other metrics like cosine distance might work better for contrastive learning?

Questions:
see above

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces ContraLSP, a novel perturbation-based explanation framework for multivariate time series models. ContraLSP jointly trains a neural perturbation network with a triplet contrastive loss to generate in-domain, counterfactual samples and learns sample-specific sparse binary masks via an ℓ₀-style regularizer tempered by a learned temporal trend for smoothness. By replacing non-salient regions with these contrastive perturbations, the method produces faithful, temporally coherent saliency maps. Empirically, the authors compare ContraLSP against multiple feature-occlusion, gradient, surrogate, and recent perturbation baselines on synthetic white-box regression, black-box classification simulations, and the MIMIC-III mortality prediction task. They report consistent improvements in precision, recall, information content, entropy, sufficiency, and comprehensiveness, supported by extensive ablation studies.

Strengths  
1. Novelty of contrastive perturbation. ContraLSP’s core idea—to integrate a triplet-based contrastive loss for generating counterfactual, in-distribution perturbations—is, to the best of my knowledge, new in the time-series explainability literature. This addresses a key shortcoming of prior perturbation-based explainers, which often produce out-of-distribution samples.  
2. Joint sparse and smooth masking. The combination of ℓ₀-regularized stochastic gates with a learned temporal trend encourages both local sparsity and temporal coherence, yielding sharper and more interpretable masks than gradient- and perturbation-only methods.  
3. Extensive empirical validation. The experimental section offers a comprehensive comparison against multiple baselines across diverse tasks and datasets, along with thorough ablation studies on contrastive loss, smoothing, distance metrics, and regularization weights. Distribution analyses further confirm that the perturbations remain in-domain.  
4. Clarity and writing quality. The paper is generally well written, with clear problem statements, algorithmic pseudocode, and illustrative figures. The related work is comprehensive, situating ContraLSP among Dynamask, Extrmask, and other recent explainers.

Weaknesses and Questions  
1. Scalability to high-dimensional or longer sequences. It remains unclear how ContraLSP scales to very high-dimensional data (e.g., video sequences or multimodal streams) or to irregularly sampled/variable-length series. A discussion or preliminary experiment in that direction would strengthen the paper.  
2. Hyperparameter sensitivity and selection. The method introduces several hyperparameters—α and β for sparsity and contrastive weighting, triplet margin b, numbers of positives/negatives (K⁺, K⁻), smoothing coefficient δ, etc. While ablations touch on some of these, the paper lacks concrete guidelines or automated strategies for selecting them. Could the authors provide heuristics or an outline of a validation procedure?  
3. Choice of distance metric. In Section 4.1, the authors use the Manhattan distance for sampling positives and negatives. It would be helpful to justify this choice over more conventional metrics such as Euclidean or cosine distance, or to report results with alternative metrics to show robustness.  
4. Computational overhead. Training a perturbation network with triplet contrastive loss and optimizing ℓ₀ gates can be resource-intensive. An explicit comparison of training time and memory usage against baselines like Dynamask or Extrmask—both in synthetic and real-world settings—would clarify practical adoption concerns.  
5. Robustness and reproducibility. How stable are the learned masks under different random seeds, model architectures (e.g., CNNs or transformers instead of RNNs), or when the underlying predictive model changes? Reporting mask variance or showing qualitative examples across runs would address this.  
6. Qualitative and user-centered evaluation. Beyond quantitative metrics, qualitative visualizations or feedback from domain experts (particularly clinicians in the MIMIC-III task) could illustrate the real-world interpretability and usefulness of the generated explanations.

Decision  
Rating: 6 – marginally above the acceptance threshold  

Overall, ContraLSP represents a technically sound and innovative contribution to time-series explainability. While the manuscript would benefit from additional discussion on scalability, hyperparameter guidance, and computational costs, the core idea of contrastive perturbations coupled with sparse, smooth masking is compelling. I recommend acceptance, subject to clarifications and minor revisions addressing the points above.

reviews.summary:
The paper presents a tool for time series explanations, which is challenging as it requires matching complex temporal patterns and features. Perturbations have been among popular approaches to identify counterfactuals, but the paper argues, in time series these can be particularly challenging since perturbations can make samples OOD, rendering the resulting explanation meaningless to the original goal (i.e., finding a counterfactual). This is especially the case when considering the label-free perturbation scenario, which is less studied in literature. 

In order to address this, the paper proposes a label-free Contrastive, and locally sparse perturbation approach that is more likely to generate in-domain perturbations. 

ContraLSP has two main components -- first a contrasting objective that seeks to ensure perturbations are dissimilar from the the original time series and are "more distracting". There is also a sparse, stochastic gate for each feature to ensure sparsity in feature selection. The final objective contains 3 terms -- contrasting loss, a regularizer on the mask, and a proximal loss to ensure predictions are close to the original, un perturbed input.
reviews.strengths:
* **Problem statement and motivation**: Time series explainability is an important topic, that has received relatively lesser attention. The paper correctly identifies the trouble with OOD perturbations in time varying data, which are poorly understood in comparison with image and language modalities. The tool is capable of working with both blackbox and whitebox models, as well as working with regression and classification tasks-- which are positives.
* **Problem formulation**: The contrasting approach to time series explainability appears to be novel as far as I know. 
* **Evaluation**: Extensive empirical evaluations are conducted on synthetic benchmarks with available ground truth on feature importance, as well as real world clinical data. ContraLSP appears to be outperforming several related baselines in both scenarios.
reviews.weaknesses:
* **Counterfactuals**: The paper generously uses counterfactuals in the text to indicate perturbations produced by their model, whereas this is a label free approach and the mask is learned to minimize the gap between the original and unperturbed samples. The contrasting objective is the only source for potential counterfactual generation, that too it is not guaranteed to do so --  this distinction should be made more explicit in the text, and reduce the usage of perturbations being called counterfactuals. The experiments mostly only measure the ability of ContraLSP on identifying salient features, so this claim should be tempered down. 
* In this context, can the authors elaborate on the failures or weaknesses of ContraLSP? Specifically, when is it expected to fail, perhaps in comparison to techniques that work with labeled data?
* **The contrasting objective** :  Since negatives are chosen at random, they are likely going to be weak negatives, and claiming these will be "more counterfactual" is probably not true. It must also be defined what "more counterfactual" means here -- more than what? How does the random negative selection ensure perturbations are crossing over class boundaries? Why is an L1 edit distance the right distance metric to do this? 
* **OOD Perturbations**  how does this objective _guarantee_ or at least ensure lack of OOD perturbations? Is the training of the perturbation function with contrastive loss sufficient to ensure this?
* **Sparse gating and stochasticity**: Please define what the heavy-tailed nature of the sparse feature selection is, and how it is relevant to ContraLSP. The hard thresholding function in eqn (3) is only needed due to the random noise injected in the masking, since $\mu'$ is a sigmoid function already.. why is the noise needed in the first place? 
* The paper's writing is not easy to follow, and this makes it hard to assess the core contribution of the work more rigorously. There are a lot of vague statements which are not stated clearly. Some of these are listed below:
	* ".. perturbation may not alleviate the distribution shift issue.." (in the abstract)
	* ".. unregulated data distribution .." (in Sec 3)
	* ".. allows perturbed features to perceive heterogenous samples, thus increasing the impact of the perturbation.." (Sec 4)
	* ".. counterfactual perturbations more distracting.." (Sec 4.1)
	* ".. due to their heavy tailed nature.." (Sec 4.2)
* **Illustrations**: I recognize time series explanations are more challenging than visual data like imagery. However, the current set of illustrations are not very clear. For e.g. in Fig 5 why is the sum of salient observations shown? What is the inference from this figure? its very unclear, please make the key observations more explicit, perhaps with the help of a simpler dataset or time series and more consistent with Fig 1, which is easier to follow.
reviews.questions: Please see above, I have listed several questions.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents a tool for time series explanations, which is challenging as it requires matching complex temporal patterns and features. Perturbations have been among popular approaches to identify counterfactuals, but the paper argues, in time series these can be particularly challenging since perturbations can make samples OOD, rendering the resulting explanation meaningless to the original goal (i.e., finding a counterfactual). This is especially the case when considering the label-free perturbation scenario, which is less studied in literature. 

In order to address this, the paper proposes a label-free Contrastive, and locally sparse perturbation approach that is more likely to generate in-domain perturbations. 

ContraLSP has two main components -- first a contrasting objective that seeks to ensure perturbations are dissimilar from the the original time series and are "more distracting". There is also a sparse, stochastic gate for each feature to ensure sparsity in feature selection. The final objective contains 3 terms -- contrasting loss, a regularizer on the mask, and a proximal loss to ensure predictions are close to the original, un perturbed input.

Strengths:
* **Problem statement and motivation**: Time series explainability is an important topic, that has received relatively lesser attention. The paper correctly identifies the trouble with OOD perturbations in time varying data, which are poorly understood in comparison with image and language modalities. The tool is capable of working with both blackbox and whitebox models, as well as working with regression and classification tasks-- which are positives.
* **Problem formulation**: The contrasting approach to time series explainability appears to be novel as far as I know. 
* **Evaluation**: Extensive empirical evaluations are conducted on synthetic benchmarks with available ground truth on feature importance, as well as real world clinical data. ContraLSP appears to be outperforming several related baselines in both scenarios.

Weaknesses:
* **Counterfactuals**: The paper generously uses counterfactuals in the text to indicate perturbations produced by their model, whereas this is a label free approach and the mask is learned to minimize the gap between the original and unperturbed samples. The contrasting objective is the only source for potential counterfactual generation, that too it is not guaranteed to do so --  this distinction should be made more explicit in the text, and reduce the usage of perturbations being called counterfactuals. The experiments mostly only measure the ability of ContraLSP on identifying salient features, so this claim should be tempered down. 
* In this context, can the authors elaborate on the failures or weaknesses of ContraLSP? Specifically, when is it expected to fail, perhaps in comparison to techniques that work with labeled data?
* **The contrasting objective** :  Since negatives are chosen at random, they are likely going to be weak negatives, and claiming these will be "more counterfactual" is probably not true. It must also be defined what "more counterfactual" means here -- more than what? How does the random negative selection ensure perturbations are crossing over class boundaries? Why is an L1 edit distance the right distance metric to do this? 
* **OOD Perturbations**  how does this objective _guarantee_ or at least ensure lack of OOD perturbations? Is the training of the perturbation function with contrastive loss sufficient to ensure this?
* **Sparse gating and stochasticity**: Please define what the heavy-tailed nature of the sparse feature selection is, and how it is relevant to ContraLSP. The hard thresholding function in eqn (3) is only needed due to the random noise injected in the masking, since $\mu'$ is a sigmoid function already.. why is the noise needed in the first place? 
* The paper's writing is not easy to follow, and this makes it hard to assess the core contribution of the work more rigorously. There are a lot of vague statements which are not stated clearly. Some of these are listed below:
	* ".. perturbation may not alleviate the distribution shift issue.." (in the abstract)
	* ".. unregulated data distribution .." (in Sec 3)
	* ".. allows perturbed features to perceive heterogenous samples, thus increasing the impact of the perturbation.." (Sec 4)
	* ".. counterfactual perturbations more distracting.." (Sec 4.1)
	* ".. due to their heavy tailed nature.." (Sec 4.2)
* **Illustrations**: I recognize time series explanations are more challenging than visual data like imagery. However, the current set of illustrations are not very clear. For e.g. in Fig 5 why is the sum of salient observations shown? What is the inference from this figure? its very unclear, please make the key observations more explicit, perhaps with the help of a simpler dataset or time series and more consistent with Fig 1, which is easier to follow.

Questions:
Please see above, I have listed several questions.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper addresses the challenging problem of explaining multivariate time series models by proposing ContraLSP, a label-free perturbation framework designed to avoid out-of-distribution (OOD) artifacts in explanations. The method consists of two main components: (1) a triplet-based contrastive objective that trains a neural perturbation network to generate in-domain “negative” examples dissimilar to the original series, and (2) locally sparse, stochastic gating with an ℓ₀-style regularizer and a learned temporal trend for smoothness. Together, these encourage perturbations that are both sparse in feature-time space and “distracting” enough to reveal salient segments, while preserving model fidelity under perturbation. Extensive evaluations on synthetic benchmarks with ground-truth saliency, black-box classification simulations, and the clinical MIMIC-III mortality prediction task demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines across metrics such as precision/recall of true salient features, information content, entropy, sufficiency, and comprehensiveness.

Strengths:  
• Importance and novelty: Time series explainability remains underexplored compared to image or text modalities. ContraLSP’s integration of contrastive perturbations with locally sparse, temporally coherent masks is, to the best of our knowledge, novel in this setting (Alternative Reviews 1–4).  
• Technical soundness: The paper clearly formulates the joint optimization of a triplet contrastive loss, a sparsity regularizer, and a proximal fidelity term. The inclusion of a learned trend function for temporal smoothness is a notable extension beyond standard perturbation or gradient-based explainers.  
• Generality: ContraLSP is applicable to both black-box and white-box models, and handles classification and regression tasks without requiring label information in training (Main Review).  
• Comprehensive evaluation: Empirical results span synthetic white-box regression, simulated black-box classification, and a real-world clinical task. Ablation studies on contrastive loss, trend smoothing, distance metrics, and regularization weights substantiate each component’s contribution (Alt. Reviews 1–4).  
• Reproducibility and impact: The release of code and detailed appendices supports reproducibility. The method’s consistent gains suggest it will be of high interest to researchers and practitioners in domains such as healthcare and finance.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. Terminology around “counterfactuals”:  
   • The manuscript frequently refers to its perturbations as counterfactuals, yet the label-free objective only minimizes prediction discrepancy rather than enforcing a true counterfactual shift across class boundaries (Main Review). Clarify this distinction throughout and temper claims about generating counterfactual instances.  
2. Contrastive objective and negative sampling:  
   • Random selection of negatives may yield weak or semantically irrelevant samples. It is unclear how this guarantees that perturbations cross meaningful decision boundaries or avoid OOD regions (Main Review, Alt. Reviews 1–4). Consider adopting more informed negative sampling (e.g., clustering beyond 2-means) or semi-supervised strategies that incorporate labels to strengthen counterfactual semantics. Explicitly define what “more counterfactual” means in context.  
3. OOD perturbation guarantee:  
   • While contrastive training encourages distribution alignment, there is no formal guarantee that perturbations remain in-domain. The paper should discuss limitations and possible failure modes—e.g., when underlying data manifolds are complex or irregularly sampled (Alt. Reviews 2–4).  
4. Sparse gating mechanism:  
   • The role of injected noise before the hard threshold (Eqn. 3) versus the sigmoid gating function requires elaboration. Explain the relevance of the claimed “heavy-tailed” nature of the sparse selection and justify design choices for ℓ₀ regularization (Main Review).  
5. Clarity and presentation:  
   • The paper contains several vague statements (e.g., “unregulated data distribution,” “perturbed features perceive heterogeneous samples”), which impede rigorous understanding (Main Review). A concise schematic of the overall training procedure, a summary table of hyperparameters, and clearer figure captions—especially for Fig. 5—would improve readability (Alt. Reviews 1–4).  
6. Hyperparameter sensitivity and computational cost:  
   • ContraLSP introduces multiple hyperparameters (α, β, margin b, K⁺, K⁻, trend weight) and relies on triplet sampling/clustering. Systematic analysis of sensitivity, guidelines for automated tuning, and comparison of training time and memory footprint against baselines such as Dynamask and Extrmask are needed (Alt. Reviews 1–4).  
7. Robustness and reproducibility:  
   • It would strengthen the paper to report the stability of explanation masks across random seeds and different underlying models (e.g., CNNs, transformers) and to discuss scalability to larger or multimodal time series (Alt. Reviews 2–4).  
8. Qualitative validation:  
   • While quantitative metrics are thorough, qualitative examples—ideally including feedback from domain experts on real clinical cases—would demonstrate practical utility and interpretability (Alt. Reviews 4).

Questions:  
1. Under what conditions or data regimes is ContraLSP expected to fail, particularly compared to label-informed or causal explanation methods?  
2. Can the authors provide heuristics or an automated procedure to select hyperparameters α and β, and to balance the triplet margin and sample counts K⁺/K⁻?  
3. What are the training and inference runtime costs relative to Dynamask and Extrmask on large datasets?  
4. How stable are the learned masks and perturbation networks under different random initializations?  
5. Have the authors considered extensions to irregularly sampled or missing-data time series, where trend estimation may be unreliable?  
6. Could a semi-supervised variant that leverages label information in the contrastive loss yield more semantically meaningful counterfactuals?

Rating: 6—marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
This work makes a valuable and timely contribution by addressing distribution shift in perturbation-based time series explanations and combining contrastive learning with sparse, temporally smoothed masks. The suggested clarifications, deeper analyses of hyperparameter sensitivity, computational overhead, and qualitative validation would further strengthen the manuscript, but they do not obscure the core novelty and solid empirical gains achieved by ContraLSP. I therefore recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary: -	The paper proposes a Contrastive and Locally Sparse Perturbations (ContraLSP) framework, which utilizes contrastive learning techniques to render non-salient features uninformative during training. The sparse gate with $\ell_0$ regularization can aid in feature selection. The proposed method exhibits strength in both with-box and black-box scenarios.
reviews.strengths:
-	The paper demonstrates its novelty by incorporating contrastive learning techniques into explainable time series tasks. Contrastive learning is a suitable solution for distinguishing informative and non-informative components.
-	The paper is well-written and easy to read, and the figures effectively aid in comprehending the main ideas.
-	Through the use of perturbation methods, ContraLSP remains relatively unaffected by noise and uninformative parts.
-	The paper shows the performance enhancement of ContraLSP across a wide range of datasets, surpassing existing methods. The authors cover various tasks in Rare-Time, Rare-Observation, and various real-world datasets.
reviews.weaknesses: -	Please refer to questions.
reviews.questions:
I will happily raise the score if the authors can address the following questions:

-	1. Although the authors discuss the selection of positive and negative samples in Appendix B, the selection of positive and negative pairs in time series is quite controversial because the proximity of data samples does not guarantee similarities [1,2,…]. The method in Appendix B appears too naive and may pose a risk of incorrect sampling for time series pair selection.
-	2. Can you provide a more specific explanation of why the counterfactual of non-salient features is superior to ignoring that part (e.g., setting it to zero), as shown in Figure 1? Even though a zero value of $x$ does not affect the training to minimize the loss of prediction with weight $w$ as $wx$, using counterfactuals can have adverse effects.
-	3. The learned mask in Figure 4 appears to exhibit similar behavior to a hard mask rather than other smooth masks. Can you clarify how the learned function $\tau(\cdot)$ behaves in a multi-dimensional context? I have read the ablation study in Table 3.
-	4. What is the difference in using the $\ell_0$ norm in Section 4.2 of your methods compared to previous methods that use the $\ell_0$ norm?

[1] Unsupervised Representation Learning for Time Series with Temporal Neighborhood Coding, ICLR 2021.
[2] TS2Vec: Towards Universal Representation of Time Series, AAAI 2022.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents ContraLSP, a novel framework for explaining multivariate time series models by jointly learning in-domain counterfactual perturbations via a triplet-based contrastive loss and locally sparse, temporally smoothed masks enforced through an ℓ₀-style regularizer. The perturbation network is trained to push non-salient regions toward distributions of negative samples while preserving model fidelity, and a learned trend function encourages temporal smoothness in the binary-skewed gating masks. The authors validate ContraLSP on synthetic white-box regression, simulated black-box classification, and the MIMIC-III clinical mortality prediction task, reporting consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines (e.g., Dynamask, Extrmask) across precision/recall of true saliency, information content, entropy, sufficiency, and comprehensiveness metrics. A suite of ablation studies further confirms the necessity of both the contrastive loss and trend-based smoothing.

Strengths:
- Novelty: ContraLSP’s integration of contrastive learning for counterfactual perturbation with sample-specific ℓ₀-regularized sparse gating and a learned temporal trend is, to our knowledge, the first such combination in time-series XAI. By explicitly aligning perturbed samples with negative distributions, the method addresses distribution-shift issues common in perturbation-based explainers.
- Empirical Thoroughness: The paper provides extensive experiments on diverse tasks—including Rare-Time and Rare-Observation synthetic settings and real-world clinical data—demonstrating robust gains over gradient-, surrogate-, and perturbation-based explainers. Alternative reviews highlight that distribution analyses and comprehensive ablations (contrastive vs. non-contrastive, smoothing vs. no smoothing, distance metrics, λ weights) give confidence in the technical soundness.
- Clarity and Presentation: The manuscript is generally well-written, with clear motivation and illustrative figures. Pseudocode and architectural diagrams aid reproducibility, and related work is covered thoroughly.
- Noise Robustness: By leveraging counterfactual perturbations rather than simple zero-out occlusions, ContraLSP remains relatively unaffected by noise and uninformative segments, yielding more faithful explanations.

Weaknesses:
- Triplet Sampling and Positive/Negative Selection: As noted in the main review, the use of proximity-based sampling for positives/negatives (Appendix B) may be too naive for general time series. Proximity does not guarantee semantic similarity [1,2], risking incorrect contrastive pairs. Alternative reviews also point out reliance on external clustering and limited discussion of sampling stability under imbalanced or large clusters.
- Hyperparameter Sensitivity: Multiple reviewers raise concerns about the sensitivity of results to hyperparameters—contrastive margin (b), numbers of positives/negatives (K⁺, K⁻), sparsity weight (α), smoothness weight (β), and triplet margin. While ablations touch on some settings, guidelines or automated tuning strategies are not provided, potentially hindering adoption.
- Computational Overhead: The triplet-based contrastive loss and ℓ₀ gate optimization introduce nontrivial training costs. None of the reviews provide detailed runtime or memory comparisons against baselines like Dynamask or Extrmask, and practitioners may be deterred by the added complexity.
- Complexity and Reproducibility: The heavy notation and deferred derivations in appendices—particularly around the learned trend function τ(·) and ℓ₀ regularization—can impede understanding. The learned mask in Figure 4 appears almost binary, raising questions about how τ(·) behaves in high-dimensional settings. A concise schematic of the overall training pipeline and more explicit pseudo-code for τ(·)'s multi-dimensional operation would improve clarity.
- Lack of Theoretical Guarantees: There is no analysis of convergence properties or optimality of the masks under the proposed objective. While empirical results are strong, some theoretical insight into mask behavior or bounds on fidelity would strengthen the work.
- Limited Qualitative Evaluation: Alternative reviews suggest that qualitative examples—particularly domain-expert feedback on clinical time series explanations—would illustrate real-world interpretability and address concerns about semantic validity of counterfactuals.

Questions and Suggestions:
1. Sampling Strategy: Can you elaborate on safeguards to ensure that proximity-based positives/negatives truly capture in-domain semantics? Have you considered label-informed or semi-supervised contrastive sampling to improve counterfactual quality?
2. Counterfactual vs. Zeroing: Why is perturbing with learned counterfactuals superior to simple occlusion (e.g., zeroing out)? Are there failure cases where counterfactuals adversely affect fidelity, as hinted in Figure 1?
3. Hyperparameter Guidance: Could you provide heuristics or an automated selection procedure for key hyperparameters (α, β, b, K⁺, K⁻) to reduce per-dataset tuning effort?
4. Computational Cost: Please report training/inference time and memory usage compared to Dynamask and Extrmask on benchmark datasets. This information would inform practitioners about scalability.
5. Mask Function Behavior: How does the learned trend τ(·) generalize in multi-dimensional time series? Does it yield smooth transitions across all features or collapse to a hard binary mask?
6. Model and Data Generalization: How does ContraLSP perform when explaining alternative architectures (e.g., CNNs, transformers) or irregularly sampled/variable-length series with missing data?
7. Stability and Reproducibility: Have you evaluated the variability of learned masks under different random seeds or retrained models? Reporting mask stability would bolster confidence in the method.

Decision:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
ContraLSP offers a compelling and novel approach to time series explainability, substantiated by thorough empirical evaluation. However, addressing the outlined concerns—particularly around sampling validity, hyperparameter sensitivity, computational overhead, and clarity—would significantly strengthen the manuscript and facilitate real-world adoption. If these questions are satisfactorily addressed in revision, I would be inclined to raise my score.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
-	The paper proposes a Contrastive and Locally Sparse Perturbations (ContraLSP) framework, which utilizes contrastive learning techniques to render non-salient features uninformative during training. The sparse gate with $\ell_0$ regularization can aid in feature selection. The proposed method exhibits strength in both with-box and black-box scenarios.

Strengths:
-	The paper demonstrates its novelty by incorporating contrastive learning techniques into explainable time series tasks. Contrastive learning is a suitable solution for distinguishing informative and non-informative components.
-	The paper is well-written and easy to read, and the figures effectively aid in comprehending the main ideas.
-	Through the use of perturbation methods, ContraLSP remains relatively unaffected by noise and uninformative parts.
-	The paper shows the performance enhancement of ContraLSP across a wide range of datasets, surpassing existing methods. The authors cover various tasks in Rare-Time, Rare-Observation, and various real-world datasets.

Weaknesses:
-	Please refer to questions.

Questions:
I will happily raise the score if the authors can address the following questions:

-	1. Although the authors discuss the selection of positive and negative samples in Appendix B, the selection of positive and negative pairs in time series is quite controversial because the proximity of data samples does not guarantee similarities [1,2,…]. The method in Appendix B appears too naive and may pose a risk of incorrect sampling for time series pair selection.
-	2. Can you provide a more specific explanation of why the counterfactual of non-salient features is superior to ignoring that part (e.g., setting it to zero), as shown in Figure 1? Even though a zero value of $x$ does not affect the training to minimize the loss of prediction with weight $w$ as $wx$, using counterfactuals can have adverse effects.
-	3. The learned mask in Figure 4 appears to exhibit similar behavior to a hard mask rather than other smooth masks. Can you clarify how the learned function $\tau(\cdot)$ behaves in a multi-dimensional context? I have read the ablation study in Table 3.
-	4. What is the difference in using the $\ell_0$ norm in Section 4.2 of your methods compared to previous methods that use the $\ell_0$ norm?

[1] Unsupervised Representation Learning for Time Series with Temporal Neighborhood Coding, ICLR 2021.
[2] TS2Vec: Towards Universal Representation of Time Series, AAAI 2022.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This manuscript presents a new method in the explainability of time series predictions. The task here is given a multivariate time series of factors to predicting an output variable to identify the regions of the input that are most predictive of the output, here defined by a binary mask. The presented technique uses a perturbative approach to compute the binary mask on the input factors. While perturbative approaches have been considered, it differs from other approaches in how it produces these perturbations. They use a contrastive (triplet) loss across samples  a smoothed sparse gate. They provide a series of experiments comparing performance to other perturbative and other approaches, such as Dynamask and Shapley Features. They use a synthetic white-box experiment with rare observation or time salience and show improved recall with their method compared to others, although all methods showed high precision. Similarly on a synthetic state-switching task they find improved recall, and perform ablations to show how  different inputs vary. They provide further examples using classification and with a real-world mortality task. There is a comprehensive supplement giving further documentation for the methods and experiments.
reviews.strengths:
* The approach is original and I believe a good contribution to existing methodologies.

* They present a comprehensive set of experiments that are well motivated and the result achieves SOTA performance on many of them. 

* While the writing and motivation is often unclear, the math behind the method is very clearly explained.
reviews.weaknesses:
* The advance feels somewhat incremental and the experiments performed are near-identical to the Dynamask paper (cited in text).

* It was unclear to me how hyperparameters were selected for each experiment. This is important as changing the expected sparsity could have a dramatic effect on recall performance. More generally there are now a family of approaches for interpretability and it is not clear what the respective strengths and weaknesses are of each. This manuscript suggests their method is superior to all others, but a discussion of which types of data each method is suited to would be helpful. 

* The manuscript is hard to follow the text as the writing and motivation is not clear in a number of points. Terms are not always introduced in order and it is hard to appreciate the innovations is. One of my reservations about this manuscript is even if the algorithm is novel it will be hard for others to appreciate. 

* In the white box experiments I had trouble appreciating the experimental design, which made it difficult to evaluate. Moreover the largest difference between methods was in the information-based metrics, which seemed to scale quite nonlinearly with recall  (fine tuning/hyperparameters).
reviews.questions:
* The authors mention treatment of inter-sample correlations as an important component of the technique, but I do not see clear evidence of this. 

* Can you explain what in-domain and negative samples refer to “Other perturbations could be either not uninformative or not in-domain, while ours is counterfactual that is toward the distribution of negative samples “

* “ To cope with it, locally stochastic gates Yang et al. (2022) consider an instance-wise selector that employs heterogeneous samples. Lee et al. (2022) takes a self-supervised way with unlabeled samples to enhance stochastic gates that encourage the model explainability meanwhile.” The terms introduced here (stochastic gates, heterogeneous samples) are not defined. The writing is unclear as well. 

* I found the description of the ‘Datasets and Benchmarks’ in 5.1 WHITE-BOX REGRESSION SIMULATION very unclear, making it hard to follow the experiments. 

* “our method significantly outperforms all other benchmarks.” I do not see any tests of significance. 

* Figure 5 I found unclear.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

title: Local Composite Saddle Point Optimization

reviews.summary: The paper proposes FeDualEx a federated primal-dual algorithm for solving distributed composite saddle point problems. The authors consider a homogeneous setting and provide convergence guarantees achieved by FeDualEx when the objective function is convex-concave. The authors also evaluate the proposed algorithm experimentally on synthetic and real datasets.
reviews.strengths: Overall. the paper is well written with the ideas clearly explained. The proposed algorithm is well-motivated and backed by strong theoretical guarantees. The experiments show the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The authors have missed an important reference [R1] which considers a nonconvex composite optimization and develops Douglas-Rachford Splitting Algorithms for solving the problem. 

- The authors should also discuss [R2] and [R3] which consider a non-convex composite problem but in a decentralized setting and without local updates. Also, in contrast to the duality-based approach taken by the authors, the works [R2] and [R3] propose primal algorithms that directly update the parameters using proximal stochastic gradient descent. The dual approach proposed by the authors is justified by the "curse of primal averaging". A question I have is why the algorithms [R2] and [R3] seem to work even though they are primal algorithms.

- Why are the guarantees presented in the paper independent of the number of clients? The effect of the number of clients should be discussed after the main results. Importantly, does the proposed algorithm achieve linear speed-up with the number of clients in the network?

- In the initial part of the paper the authors refer to the distance-generating function to be strictly convex but later it is assumed to be strongly convex. It is advisable to call it strongly convex from the beginning. 

- Define $h_1$, $h_2$ in Definition 3. 

- After Definition 3, the authors mention that the previous approaches that add the composite term to the Bregman
divergence may not work for dual extrapolation as certain parts of the analysis break down. Can the authors be more specific about what they mean here?

[R1] Dinh et al., FedDR – Randomized Douglas-Rachford Splitting Algorithms for Nonconvex Federated Composite Optimization, 2021(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2103.03452.pdf)

[R2] Yan et al., Compressed Decentralized Proximal Stochastic Gradient Method for Nonconvex Composite Problems with Heterogeneous Data, 2023 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.14252.pdf)

[R3] Xiao et al., A One-Sample Decentralized Proximal Algorithm for Non-Convex Stochastic Composite Optimization, 2023 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.09766.pdf)
reviews.questions: See the weaknesses section above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
The paper proposes FeDualEx a federated primal-dual algorithm for solving distributed composite saddle point problems. The authors consider a homogeneous setting and provide convergence guarantees achieved by FeDualEx when the objective function is convex-concave. The authors also evaluate the proposed algorithm experimentally on synthetic and real datasets.

Strengths:
Overall. the paper is well written with the ideas clearly explained. The proposed algorithm is well-motivated and backed by strong theoretical guarantees. The experiments show the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

Weaknesses:
- The authors have missed an important reference [R1] which considers a nonconvex composite optimization and develops Douglas-Rachford Splitting Algorithms for solving the problem. 

- The authors should also discuss [R2] and [R3] which consider a non-convex composite problem but in a decentralized setting and without local updates. Also, in contrast to the duality-based approach taken by the authors, the works [R2] and [R3] propose primal algorithms that directly update the parameters using proximal stochastic gradient descent. The dual approach proposed by the authors is justified by the "curse of primal averaging". A question I have is why the algorithms [R2] and [R3] seem to work even though they are primal algorithms.

- Why are the guarantees presented in the paper independent of the number of clients? The effect of the number of clients should be discussed after the main results. Importantly, does the proposed algorithm achieve linear speed-up with the number of clients in the network?

- In the initial part of the paper the authors refer to the distance-generating function to be strictly convex but later it is assumed to be strongly convex. It is advisable to call it strongly convex from the beginning. 

- Define $h_1$, $h_2$ in Definition 3. 

- After Definition 3, the authors mention that the previous approaches that add the composite term to the Bregman
divergence may not work for dual extrapolation as certain parts of the analysis break down. Can the authors be more specific about what they mean here?

[R1] Dinh et al., FedDR – Randomized Douglas-Rachford Splitting Algorithms for Nonconvex Federated Composite Optimization, 2021(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2103.03452.pdf)

[R2] Yan et al., Compressed Decentralized Proximal Stochastic Gradient Method for Nonconvex Composite Problems with Heterogeneous Data, 2023 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.14252.pdf)

[R3] Xiao et al., A One-Sample Decentralized Proximal Algorithm for Non-Convex Stochastic Composite Optimization, 2023 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.09766.pdf)

See the weaknesses section above.
